title: Cybersecurity Internship
description: A 3-month structured cybersecurity internship covering foundations, intermediate security analysis, and advanced penetration testing.

months.title: Cybersecurity Foundations
months.goal: Build a strong understanding of networking, Linux, and web security fundamentals.

days.title: Introduction to Cybersecurity & Types of Hackers

content.theory:
Cybersecurity is the practice of protecting computers, mobile devices, servers, networks, and digital data from unauthorized access, attacks, damage, or theft. In today’s world, almost everything is connected to the internet — banking, shopping, communication, education, healthcare, transportation, and even government systems. Because of this, protecting digital systems has become one of the most important responsibilities for individuals, companies, and governments.

Every time we use a smartphone, browse websites, log into social media, make online payments, or store files in the cloud, data is being shared across networks. If these systems are not protected properly, attackers can steal sensitive information such as passwords, credit card details, personal photos, business records, or confidential government information.

Cybersecurity focuses on preventing these attacks and ensuring that systems remain secure, reliable, and available. The main goals of cybersecurity are:

1. Confidentiality – Ensuring that sensitive information is only accessible to authorized users.
2. Integrity – Ensuring that data is not modified or tampered with without permission.
3. Availability – Ensuring that systems and services remain accessible when needed.

Cyber attacks can happen in many forms. Some common examples include:

• Malware Attacks – Malicious software such as viruses, worms, trojans, and ransomware designed to damage or control systems.
• Phishing Attacks – Fake emails or websites that trick users into sharing passwords or financial information.
• Password Attacks – Attempts to crack or steal passwords using different methods.
• Denial of Service (DoS) Attacks – Flooding a system with traffic to make it unavailable.
• Social Engineering – Manipulating people into revealing confidential information.

As technology grows, cyber threats are also increasing rapidly. Organizations around the world spend billions of dollars every year on cybersecurity because a single attack can cause financial loss, reputation damage, legal issues, and loss of customer trust.

To protect systems from attacks, cybersecurity professionals use different security measures such as:

• Firewalls
• Antivirus software
• Encryption
• Multi-factor authentication
• Security monitoring tools
• Regular software updates
• Security awareness training

Cybersecurity is not only the responsibility of companies or security experts. Every individual who uses the internet should follow basic cyber safety practices such as:

• Creating strong passwords
• Avoiding suspicious links and emails
• Updating software regularly
• Using secure Wi-Fi networks
• Enabling two-factor authentication
• Not sharing personal information carelessly online

One of the most interesting areas in cybersecurity is ethical hacking. Ethical hackers help organizations identify weaknesses before malicious attackers can exploit them. They test systems legally and provide solutions to improve security.

Hackers are individuals who use technical skills to interact with computer systems and networks. Not all hackers are criminals. Hackers are categorized based on their intentions, methods, and legality.

1. Black Hat Hackers
Black Hat Hackers are malicious attackers who illegally break into systems for personal gain, financial theft, espionage, or destruction. They may steal sensitive information, install malware, or demand ransom payments. These hackers violate laws and cause harm to individuals and organizations.

Examples of black hat activities include:
• Stealing bank information
• Spreading ransomware
• Hacking social media accounts
• Selling stolen data on the dark web

2. White Hat Hackers (Ethical Hackers)
White Hat Hackers are cybersecurity professionals who use hacking skills legally and ethically. They work with organizations to identify vulnerabilities and improve security. Ethical hackers perform penetration testing, vulnerability assessments, and security audits with proper authorization.

Their responsibilities include:
• Finding vulnerabilities
• Testing network security
• Securing applications
• Reporting security weaknesses
• Helping prevent future attacks

White hat hackers play a major role in protecting businesses and users from cyber threats.

3. Grey Hat Hackers
Grey Hat Hackers operate between ethical and unethical boundaries. They may access systems without permission but usually do not intend to cause damage. Sometimes they discover vulnerabilities and inform organizations about them. However, accessing systems without authorization is still illegal.

4. Script Kiddies
Script Kiddies are inexperienced individuals who use existing hacking tools and scripts created by others without understanding how they work. They often attack systems for fun, attention, or curiosity rather than serious criminal intentions.

Although they may not have advanced technical knowledge, script kiddies can still cause damage using publicly available tools.

5. Hacktivists
Hacktivists are hackers motivated by political, religious, or social causes. Instead of financial gain, they target organizations or governments to spread messages or protest against certain actions.

Common hacktivist activities include:
• Website defacement
• Data leaks
• Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks
• Online protests

6. Nation-State Hackers
Nation-state hackers are highly skilled attackers supported by governments. Their goals may include cyber warfare, spying, intelligence gathering, or disrupting another country's infrastructure.

These attacks often target:
• Government agencies
• Military systems
• Financial institutions
• Critical infrastructure
• Research organizations

Nation-state attacks are considered among the most dangerous cyber threats because they are highly organized and well-funded.

7. Insider Threats
Insider threats come from employees, contractors, or trusted individuals within an organization who misuse their access intentionally or accidentally. Insider threats are dangerous because insiders already have access to sensitive systems and information.

Examples include:
• Stealing company data
• Sharing confidential information
• Accidentally exposing sensitive files
• Misusing access privileges

Cybersecurity is a constantly evolving field because attackers continuously develop new techniques. Professionals in this field must regularly update their knowledge, learn new tools, and stay aware of modern threats.

Today, cybersecurity offers many career opportunities such as:
• Ethical Hacker
• Security Analyst
• Penetration Tester
• Security Engineer
• Digital Forensics Expert
• SOC Analyst
• Malware Analyst
• Cloud Security Engineer

Cybersecurity has become essential for protecting modern digital life. Understanding the basics of cybersecurity and different types of hackers is the first step toward building strong security knowledge and becoming a responsible technology user or cybersecurity professional.
keyPoints: Cybersecurity protects systems, networks, and data from cyber attacks
The three main goals of cybersecurity are Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability
Cyber attacks include malware, phishing, password attacks, and denial of service attacks
Black Hat Hackers perform illegal activities for personal or financial gain
White Hat Hackers work legally to identify and fix security vulnerabilities
Grey Hat Hackers operate between ethical and unethical boundaries
Script Kiddies use pre-made hacking tools without deep technical knowledge
Hacktivists perform attacks for political or social reasons
Nation-State Hackers are government-sponsored cyber attackers
Insider Threats come from trusted individuals misusing access
Strong passwords, software updates, and two-factor authentication improve security
Cybersecurity is one of the fastest-growing career fields in technology
resources: Introduction to Ethical Hacking articles and videos

days.title: CIA Triad — The Core of Security

content.theory:
The CIA Triad is one of the most important and fundamental concepts in cybersecurity and information security. It represents the three core principles that every secure system should follow: Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability. These three principles form the foundation of security policies, technologies, and practices used by organizations around the world.

The CIA Triad helps security professionals understand what needs to be protected and how to design systems that are secure, reliable, and trustworthy. Every cybersecurity strategy, whether for a small company or a large government organization, is built around these three pillars.

The term “CIA” in cybersecurity does not refer to the Central Intelligence Agency. Instead, it stands for:

• C – Confidentiality
• I – Integrity
• A – Availability

The CIA Triad is used to evaluate security risks, identify vulnerabilities, and implement proper security controls. If even one pillar is weak, the entire security system can become vulnerable.

1. Confidentiality
Confidentiality means protecting sensitive information from unauthorized access. Only authorized users should be able to view or access specific data.

In today’s digital world, organizations store huge amounts of sensitive information such as:

• Personal information
• Passwords
• Banking details
• Medical records
• Business secrets
• Government documents

If unauthorized individuals gain access to this information, it can result in data breaches, identity theft, financial loss, and damage to reputation.

To maintain confidentiality, organizations use several security techniques such as:

• Password protection
• Multi-factor authentication (MFA)
• Encryption
• Access control systems
• Biometrics
• User permissions

Example:
Imagine a hospital database containing patient records. Only doctors, nurses, and authorized staff should be able to access patient information. If a hacker steals patient data, confidentiality has been violated.

Real-World Example:
A phishing attack that steals employee login credentials can allow attackers to access confidential company information.

2. Integrity
Integrity refers to maintaining the accuracy, consistency, and trustworthiness of data. Data should not be modified, deleted, or altered by unauthorized users.

Integrity ensures that information remains correct and reliable throughout its lifecycle. Even a small change in important data can create serious problems.

For example:

• A student’s exam marks should not be changed illegally
• Bank transaction records should remain accurate
• Medical prescriptions should not be modified
• Business reports should remain authentic

Cyber attackers may attempt to alter data to create confusion, commit fraud, or damage systems.

Security measures used to maintain integrity include:

• Hashing
• Checksums
• Digital signatures
• File integrity monitoring
• Version control
• Database controls

Example:
Suppose an attacker changes the amount in a bank transaction from ₹1,000 to ₹1,00,000. The data is no longer accurate, meaning integrity has been compromised.

Real-World Example:
If a cybercriminal modifies a company’s financial records, it can lead to incorrect business decisions and financial fraud.

3. Availability
Availability ensures that systems, services, and data remain accessible to authorized users whenever needed.

Even if data is confidential and accurate, it becomes useless if users cannot access it when required.

Availability is extremely important for:

• Hospitals
• Banking systems
• E-commerce websites
• Emergency services
• Government services
• Cloud platforms

Cyber attacks, hardware failures, software crashes, power outages, or natural disasters can affect system availability.

Organizations use various methods to maintain availability such as:

• Regular backups
• Disaster recovery plans
• Redundant servers
• Cloud infrastructure
• Load balancing
• DDoS protection
• Power backup systems

Example:
An online shopping website becomes unavailable during a major sale because of a Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack. Customers cannot place orders, meaning availability has been affected.

Real-World Example:
If a hospital server goes offline during an emergency, doctors may not be able to access patient records, which can create life-threatening situations.

Importance of the CIA Triad
The CIA Triad is important because it helps organizations build balanced and effective security systems. Focusing on only one pillar is not enough.

For example:

• A system may have strong confidentiality but poor availability if it crashes often.
• A highly available system without integrity controls can spread incorrect data.
• A system with integrity and availability but weak confidentiality can leak sensitive information.

Security professionals must ensure all three pillars are properly maintained.

Additional Concepts Related to the CIA Triad
Over time, cybersecurity evolved beyond the three basic pillars, and additional concepts were introduced.

1. Non-Repudiation
Non-repudiation ensures that a person cannot deny performing a particular action.

For example:

• A sender cannot deny sending an email
• A customer cannot deny making an online payment
• A user cannot deny logging into a system

Technologies used for non-repudiation include:

• Digital signatures
• Audit logs
• Transaction records
• Certificates

2. Authentication
Authentication is the process of verifying the identity of a user.

Examples include:

• Username and password
• OTP verification
• Fingerprint scanning
• Face recognition

3. Authorization
Authorization determines what an authenticated user is allowed to access.

Example:
An employee may log into a company system but only access files related to their department.

4. Accounting (Auditing)
Accounting tracks and records user activities within a system.

Examples include:

• Login history
• File access records
• System logs
• Security monitoring

These three concepts together form the AAA model:

• Authentication
• Authorization
• Accounting

The AAA model extends the CIA Triad by adding better user management and monitoring capabilities.

Real-World Example of the CIA Triad
Consider a hospital management system:

Confidentiality:
Patient records should only be accessible to authorized medical staff.

Integrity:
Medical reports and prescriptions should remain accurate and unchanged.

Availability:
Doctors and nurses must be able to access patient data 24/7 during emergencies.

If any one of these pillars fails, patient care and hospital operations can be severely affected.

Another Example — Online Banking:

Confidentiality:
Bank account details and passwords must remain private.

Integrity:
Transaction amounts and account balances must remain accurate.

Availability:
Customers should be able to access online banking services anytime.

Threats Against the CIA Triad
Different cyber attacks target different pillars of the CIA Triad.

Threats to Confidentiality:
• Phishing attacks
• Password theft
• Data breaches
• Spyware

Threats to Integrity:
• Malware
• Unauthorized file modification
• SQL injection attacks
• Data tampering

Threats to Availability:
• DDoS attacks
• Server failures
• Ransomware
• Power outages

Understanding the CIA Triad helps cybersecurity professionals identify risks and apply the right protection methods.

The CIA Triad remains one of the most important models in cybersecurity because it provides a simple yet powerful framework for protecting information systems. Whether designing a website, securing a company network, or protecting cloud data, security experts always focus on confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

A strong cybersecurity system is one that keeps information private, accurate, and accessible whenever needed.
keyPoints: CIA stands for Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability
The CIA Triad is the foundation of information security
Confidentiality protects data from unauthorized access
Integrity ensures data remains accurate and unmodified
Availability ensures systems and data remain accessible
Encryption and access controls help maintain confidentiality
Hashing and digital signatures help maintain integrity
Backups and redundancy help maintain availability
Non-repudiation prevents users from denying actions they performed
Authentication verifies identity
Authorization controls what users can access
Accounting records and monitors user activities
Different cyber attacks target different CIA pillars
The CIA Triad is used in almost every cybersecurity framework and policy

days.title: Networking Basics — How the Internet Works

content.theory:
Networking is one of the most important foundations of cybersecurity because every cyber attack travels through a network. A computer network is a collection of interconnected devices that communicate and share resources with each other. These devices may include computers, smartphones, servers, routers, switches, printers, IoT devices, and many others. The internet itself is the world’s largest computer network.

Whenever we open a website, send messages, stream videos, use social media, or download files, our devices communicate with other systems through networks. Understanding networking is essential in cybersecurity because attackers use networks to perform attacks, while defenders use networking knowledge to detect, monitor, and stop those attacks.

For example, when a user types google.com into a browser, the request travels through multiple devices and networks before reaching Google’s server. The server then sends a response back to the user’s browser. This entire process happens in milliseconds.

To understand communication clearly, networking models were created. The most important model is the OSI Model.

The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Model is a conceptual framework developed by ISO to explain how devices communicate over networks. It divides communication into seven layers, where each layer performs a specific function.

The seven layers of the OSI model are:

1. Physical Layer
2. Data Link Layer
3. Network Layer
4. Transport Layer
5. Session Layer
6. Presentation Layer
7. Application Layer

The OSI model helps students and cybersecurity professionals understand how data flows from one device to another.

Layer 1 — Physical Layer:
The Physical Layer is responsible for transmitting raw bits through physical media such as cables, wireless signals, and fiber optics. It deals with electrical signals, connectors, hardware devices, and communication media.

Examples include Ethernet cables, Wi-Fi signals, switches, repeaters, and hubs.

This layer is important because without physical transmission, communication cannot happen.

Cybersecurity relevance:
Attackers may physically connect unauthorized devices, damage cables, or intercept wireless communication.

Layer 2 — Data Link Layer:
The Data Link Layer handles communication between devices within the same local network. It is responsible for framing, error detection, MAC addressing, and local delivery.

A MAC (Media Access Control) address is a unique hardware address assigned to a network interface card.

Example MAC Address:
00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E

Devices operating at this layer include switches and network interface cards.

Switches use MAC addresses to forward data only to the correct device.

Cybersecurity relevance:
Common attacks include MAC spoofing and ARP spoofing.

Layer 3 — Network Layer:
The Network Layer handles IP addressing, routing, and packet forwarding between networks.

Routers operate at this layer.

An IP address is a logical address used to identify devices on a network.

Example IPv4 Address:
192.168.1.1

Without IP addresses, devices cannot communicate across networks.

Cybersecurity relevance:
Attackers may use IP spoofing to hide their identity.

Layer 4 — Transport Layer:
The Transport Layer ensures reliable communication between systems. It handles segmentation, error checking, flow control, and delivery reliability.

Two major protocols operate here:

TCP (Transmission Control Protocol):
TCP is reliable and connection-oriented. It guarantees packet delivery and maintains proper communication.

TCP is used in:
• Websites
• Emails
• File transfers
• Banking systems

TCP uses the Three-Way Handshake:
1. SYN
2. SYN-ACK
3. ACK

UDP (User Datagram Protocol):
UDP is faster but less reliable. It is connectionless and does not guarantee delivery.

UDP is used in:
• Gaming
• Video streaming
• VoIP calls

Ports also operate at this layer.

Common ports:
• Port 80 — HTTP
• Port 443 — HTTPS
• Port 22 — SSH
• Port 21 — FTP
• Port 53 — DNS

Cybersecurity relevance:
Port scanning and DDoS attacks target this layer.

Layer 5 — Session Layer:
The Session Layer manages communication sessions between applications.

Responsibilities include:
• Session establishment
• Session maintenance
• Session termination

Example:
Keeping users logged into websites.

Cybersecurity relevance:
Session hijacking attacks target this layer.

Layer 6 — Presentation Layer:
The Presentation Layer handles encryption, compression, encoding, and data translation.

Examples:
• SSL/TLS
• JPEG
• MP4

HTTPS encryption works at this layer.

This layer ensures systems understand data properly.

Cybersecurity relevance:
Encryption protects sensitive communication.

Layer 7 — Application Layer:
The Application Layer is the topmost layer and interacts directly with users.

Protocols operating here include:
• HTTP/HTTPS
• FTP
• DNS
• SMTP
• SSH

Applications include:
• Browsers
• Email applications
• Websites
• Messaging applications

Most web attacks occur at this layer.

Examples include:
• SQL Injection
• Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
• Phishing

Apart from the OSI model, the TCP/IP model is another important networking model.

The TCP/IP model is the practical model used by the internet. It has four layers:

1. Application Layer
2. Transport Layer
3. Internet Layer
4. Network Access Layer

The TCP/IP model is simpler and focuses on real-world internet communication.

IP Addressing:
An IP address uniquely identifies devices on a network.

IPv4:
IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses.

Example:
192.168.1.1

IPv4 addresses are limited in number.

IPv6:
IPv6 was introduced to solve IPv4 exhaustion.

IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses.

Example:
2001:db8::1

Advantages of IPv6:
• Huge address space
• Better routing
• Better efficiency
• Improved security support

Public vs Private IP Addresses:

Public IP:
Accessible over the internet and assigned by ISPs.

Private IP:
Used inside local networks.

Private IP ranges:
• 10.0.0.0 – 10.255.255.255
• 172.16.0.0 – 172.31.255.255
• 192.168.0.0 – 192.168.255.255

Private IP addresses are not directly routable on the internet.

NAT (Network Address Translation):
NAT allows multiple devices inside a private network to share a single public IP address.

For example, your phone, laptop, and smart TV may all access the internet using one public IP.

Advantages of NAT:
• Conserves IPv4 addresses
• Adds basic privacy
• Reduces direct exposure to the internet

Subnetting:
Subnetting divides a large network into smaller sub-networks.

Benefits:
• Better performance
• Easier management
• Reduced congestion
• Improved security

Example subnet mask:
255.255.255.0

CIDR notation:
/24

DNS (Domain Name System):
DNS converts domain names into IP addresses.

Humans remember names like google.com while computers communicate using IP addresses.

DNS acts like the phonebook of the internet.

DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol):
DHCP automatically assigns:
• IP addresses
• Subnet masks
• Default gateways
• DNS servers

Without DHCP, users would configure network settings manually.

ARP (Address Resolution Protocol):
ARP converts IP addresses into MAC addresses within local networks.

Networking Devices:

Router:
Routers connect different networks and forward packets between them.

Switch:
Switches connect devices inside a LAN and use MAC addresses for efficient communication.

Hub:
Hubs broadcast data to all connected devices and are less secure.

Firewall:
A firewall monitors and filters incoming and outgoing traffic.

Firewalls help:
• Block attackers
• Prevent unauthorized access
• Monitor traffic

Common Network Attacks:

1. DDoS Attack:
Overwhelms servers with traffic.

2. Man-in-the-Middle (MITM):
Intercepts communication between users.

3. ARP Spoofing:
Manipulates ARP tables.

4. DNS Spoofing:
Redirects users to fake websites.

5. Packet Sniffing:
Captures network traffic.

Networking is extremely important in cybersecurity because every cyber attack uses networks in some form. Understanding networking helps cybersecurity professionals secure systems, analyze attacks, monitor traffic, and protect organizations from threats.
keyPoints: The OSI model has 7 layers
The TCP/IP model has 4 layers
TCP provides reliable communication while UDP focuses on speed
IP addresses identify devices on networks
IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses while IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses
Private IP addresses are used inside local networks
NAT allows multiple devices to share one public IP
DNS converts domain names into IP addresses
DHCP automatically assigns network settings
ARP maps IP addresses to MAC addresses
Routers connect networks while switches connect devices in LANs
Firewalls filter and secure network traffic
Most cyber attacks exploit networking vulnerabilities

content.theory:
Whenever we open a website in a browser, multiple networking technologies work together in the background to establish communication between our device and the web server. Two of the most important concepts involved in this process are DNS and HTTP/HTTPS.

DNS helps users reach websites using domain names instead of IP addresses, while HTTP and HTTPS are protocols responsible for transferring web data between browsers and servers.

Understanding DNS and HTTP/HTTPS is extremely important in cybersecurity because attackers often target these technologies to intercept communication, redirect users, steal credentials, or perform phishing attacks.

============================
DNS (Domain Name System)
============================

The Domain Name System (DNS) is often called the “phonebook of the internet.”

Humans remember names such as:

• google.com
• youtube.com
• amazon.com

But computers communicate using IP addresses.

Example:
142.250.xxx.xxx

DNS converts human-readable domain names into IP addresses.

Without DNS, users would need to memorize numerical IP addresses for every website.

============================
Why DNS is Important
============================

DNS makes internet browsing simple and user-friendly.

Instead of typing:

142.250.183.14

Users simply type:

google.com

DNS automatically finds the correct IP address.

DNS is extremely important because almost every internet service depends on it.

Examples:

• Websites
• Email systems
• Cloud applications
• Online games
• APIs

============================
DNS Resolution Process
============================

When a user enters a website name into a browser, a DNS resolution process begins.

The steps are:

1. Browser Cache Check
The browser first checks whether it already knows the IP address.

2. Recursive Resolver
If not found, the request goes to a recursive DNS resolver, usually provided by the ISP.

3. Root DNS Server
The resolver asks the root server where the requested domain exists.

4. TLD Server
The root server responds with the address of the Top-Level Domain (TLD) server.

Examples of TLDs:

• .com
• .org
• .net
• .edu

5. Authoritative Name Server
The TLD server directs the resolver to the authoritative DNS server containing the actual IP address.

6. IP Address Returned
The authoritative server returns the IP address.

7. Browser Connects to Server
The browser now connects to the website server using that IP address.

This process usually happens within milliseconds.

============================
DNS Record Types
============================

DNS records store information about domains.

Common DNS records include:

1. A Record
Maps a domain to an IPv4 address.

Example:
example.com → 192.168.1.1

2. AAAA Record
Maps a domain to an IPv6 address.

3. CNAME Record
Used as an alias for another domain.

Example:
www.example.com → example.com

4. MX Record
Defines mail servers for email delivery.

5. TXT Record
Stores text-based information.

Used for:

• Domain verification
• SPF records
• Security policies

6. NS Record
Defines authoritative nameservers for a domain.

============================
DNS Attacks
============================

Because DNS is critical, attackers often target it.

1. DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning
Attackers inject fake DNS records into cache memory.

Result:
Users are redirected to fake websites.

Example:
A user types bank.com but lands on a phishing website.

2. DNS Hijacking
Attackers change DNS settings to redirect users.

3. DNS Amplification Attack
A DDoS attack where attackers abuse DNS servers to flood victims with traffic.

4. DNS Tunneling
Attackers hide malicious communication inside DNS traffic.

Cybersecurity professionals monitor DNS traffic carefully because abnormal DNS activity may indicate malware or data exfiltration.

============================
HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)
============================

HTTP is the protocol used for transferring web pages and data between browsers and web servers.

When you open a website, your browser sends HTTP requests to the server.

The server responds with:

• Web pages
• Images
• Videos
• APIs
• Files

HTTP works on:

Port 80

============================
How HTTP Works
============================

The communication process:

1. Browser sends request.
2. Server receives request.
3. Server processes it.
4. Server sends response.
5. Browser displays content.

HTTP follows a request-response model.

============================
Problem with HTTP
============================

HTTP sends data in plain text.

This means:

• Usernames
• Passwords
• Cookies
• Sensitive data

Can be intercepted and read by attackers.

Anyone monitoring the network traffic can view the communication.

Because of this security problem, HTTPS was introduced.

============================
HTTPS (HyperText Transfer Protocol Secure)
============================

HTTPS is the secure version of HTTP.

HTTPS uses encryption through TLS/SSL.

HTTPS works on:

Port 443

HTTPS protects:

• Login credentials
• Banking information
• Personal data
• Cookies
• API communication

When HTTPS is enabled, communication becomes encrypted.

Even if attackers intercept traffic, they cannot easily read the data.

============================
Importance of HTTPS
============================

HTTPS provides:

1. Confidentiality
Protects data from unauthorized viewing.

2. Integrity
Prevents attackers from modifying data during transmission.

3. Authentication
Verifies website identity using certificates.

Without HTTPS, attackers on public Wi-Fi networks could steal sensitive information.

============================
TLS/SSL
============================

TLS (Transport Layer Security) and SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) are cryptographic protocols used to secure communication.

SSL is older and mostly replaced by TLS.

HTTPS uses TLS encryption.

TLS ensures:

• Secure communication
• Encrypted transmission
• Identity verification

============================
TLS Handshake
============================

Before encrypted communication begins, a TLS handshake occurs.

Steps:

1. Client Hello
The browser sends supported TLS versions and encryption methods.

2. Server Hello
The server responds with the chosen encryption method and SSL certificate.

3. Certificate Verification
The browser verifies the certificate.

4. Key Exchange
Encryption keys are securely exchanged.

5. Secure Communication Begins
All future communication becomes encrypted.

This process happens very quickly.

============================
SSL Certificates
============================

SSL certificates verify the identity of websites.

Certificates are issued by Certificate Authorities (CAs).

Popular CAs:

• Let's Encrypt
• DigiCert
• GlobalSign

Certificates contain:

• Domain name
• Public key
• Expiration date
• Issuer details

When browsers trust the certificate, they display the padlock icon.

============================
HTTP Methods
============================

HTTP methods define actions performed on resources.

1. GET
Retrieves data.

Example:
Opening a webpage.

2. POST
Sends data to the server.

Example:
Submitting login forms.

3. PUT
Updates existing resources.

4. DELETE
Deletes resources.

5. PATCH
Partially updates resources.

============================
Security Importance of HTTP Methods
============================

Improperly secured HTTP methods can create vulnerabilities.

Examples:

• Unprotected DELETE methods may allow attackers to remove data.
• Weak authentication on PUT requests may allow unauthorized modifications.
• Sensitive data inside GET URLs may leak information.

Cybersecurity professionals analyze HTTP methods during penetration testing.

============================
HTTP Status Codes
============================

Servers return status codes in responses.

Common status codes:

200 — Success
301 — Redirect
403 — Forbidden
404 — Not Found
500 — Internal Server Error

These help developers and security analysts identify issues.

============================
HTTP Headers
============================

HTTP headers provide additional information during communication.

Security-related headers include:

• Content-Security-Policy (CSP)
• X-Frame-Options
• Strict-Transport-Security
• X-Content-Type-Options

Proper security headers help reduce web vulnerabilities.

============================
HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security)
============================

HSTS forces browsers to always use HTTPS.

Benefits:

• Prevents downgrade attacks
• Prevents insecure HTTP communication
• Improves website security

Without HSTS, attackers may force users onto insecure HTTP connections.

============================
Common Attacks Related to HTTP/HTTPS
============================

1. Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)
Attackers intercept communication.

2. SSL Stripping
Attackers downgrade HTTPS connections to HTTP.

3. Session Hijacking
Attackers steal session cookies.

4. Phishing
Fake HTTPS websites trick users.

5. Packet Sniffing
Attackers capture unencrypted HTTP traffic.

============================
DNS and HTTPS in Cybersecurity
============================

Cybersecurity professionals monitor:

• DNS traffic
• TLS certificates
• HTTP requests
• Security headers
• Suspicious domains

Many attacks such as phishing, malware delivery, command-and-control communication, and data exfiltration rely heavily on DNS and HTTP communication.

Understanding DNS and HTTPS is essential for:

• Ethical Hacking
• Penetration Testing
• Web Security
• SOC Operations
• Threat Hunting
• Malware Analysis

Modern internet security depends heavily on secure DNS resolution and encrypted HTTPS communication.
keyPoints: DNS converts domain names into IP addresses
DNS resolution involves root, TLD, and authoritative servers
HTTP transfers data in plain text using port 80
HTTPS uses TLS/SSL encryption on port 443
HTTPS provides confidentiality, integrity, and authentication
TLS handshake establishes secure encrypted communication
SSL certificates verify website identity
HTTP methods include GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, and PATCH
HSTS forces secure HTTPS communication
DNS spoofing and cache poisoning redirect users to fake websites
HTTP headers improve web security
Many cyber attacks target DNS and HTTP communication
resources: RFC Documentation for HTTP and DNS

days.title: Ports, Protocols & Setting Up Your Lab

content.theory:
In computer networking, communication between devices happens through ports and protocols. Ports act like doors or communication endpoints on a computer system, while protocols define the rules used for communication.

Understanding ports and protocols is extremely important in cybersecurity because attackers often scan systems for open ports and vulnerable services. Ethical hackers and penetration testers also use port scanning to identify exposed services and security weaknesses.

Every application or service running on a system communicates using specific ports.

For example:

• Web servers use port 80 or 443
• SSH services use port 22
• DNS services use port 53

When data reaches a computer, the operating system uses port numbers to determine which application should receive the data.

============================
What is a Port?
============================

A port is a logical communication endpoint used by networking applications.

Ports help computers identify which service or application should receive incoming data.

For example:

• Websites use ports
• Email services use ports
• Remote login services use ports
• Database servers use ports

Ports work together with IP addresses.

IP Address:
Identifies the device.

Port Number:
Identifies the specific service on the device.

Example:
192.168.1.10:80

Here:

• 192.168.1.10 → Device IP
• 80 → Web service port

Without ports, computers would not know which application should receive network traffic.

============================
Port Number Ranges
============================

Ports are divided into three major ranges.

1. Well-Known Ports (0–1023)
These are reserved for common protocols and services.

Examples:

• 80 — HTTP
• 443 — HTTPS
• 22 — SSH
• 53 — DNS

2. Registered Ports (1024–49151)
These are used by user applications and registered services.

Examples:

• 3306 — MySQL
• 5432 — PostgreSQL
• 8080 — Alternative HTTP

3. Dynamic / Ephemeral Ports (49152–65535)
These are temporary ports automatically assigned by operating systems during communication.

Example:
When your browser connects to a website, your system may temporarily use an ephemeral port.

============================
Important Ports Every Cybersecurity Student Should Know
============================

Port 20/21 — FTP (File Transfer Protocol)
Used for transferring files between systems.

Problem:
FTP sends usernames and passwords in plain text.

Cybersecurity risk:
Attackers can sniff credentials.

============================
Port 22 — SSH (Secure Shell)
============================

SSH provides secure remote access to systems.

Features:

• Encrypted communication
• Secure login
• Remote command execution

Importance:
SSH is widely used by system administrators.

Cybersecurity relevance:
Attackers often target weak SSH passwords.

============================
Port 23 — Telnet
============================

Telnet provides remote access similar to SSH.

Problem:
Telnet is not encrypted.

Everything including passwords is sent in plain text.

Because of this, Telnet is considered insecure.

Cybersecurity relevance:
Telnet is commonly exploited on old systems and IoT devices.

============================
Port 25 — SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol)
============================

SMTP is used for sending emails.

Mail servers use SMTP to transfer email messages.

Cybersecurity relevance:
Attackers abuse SMTP servers for spam and phishing campaigns.

============================
Port 53 — DNS (Domain Name System)
============================

DNS converts domain names into IP addresses.

Example:

google.com → IP address

Cybersecurity relevance:
DNS attacks include:

• DNS spoofing
• DNS tunneling
• DNS amplification attacks

============================
Port 80 — HTTP
============================

HTTP is used for web communication.

Problem:
HTTP sends data in plain text.

Cybersecurity relevance:
Attackers can intercept unencrypted communication.

============================
Port 443 — HTTPS
============================

HTTPS is the secure version of HTTP.

Uses:

• TLS/SSL encryption
• Secure communication
• Data protection

Cybersecurity relevance:
HTTPS protects sensitive information such as passwords and banking data.

============================
Port 110 — POP3
============================

POP3 is used for retrieving emails.

It downloads emails from mail servers.

============================
Port 143 — IMAP
============================

IMAP is also used for retrieving emails.

Unlike POP3, IMAP synchronizes emails across multiple devices.

============================
Port 445 — SMB (Server Message Block)
============================

SMB is used for:

• File sharing
• Printer sharing
• Windows networking

Cybersecurity relevance:
Many ransomware attacks target SMB vulnerabilities.

Example:
WannaCry ransomware exploited SMB vulnerabilities.

============================
Port 3306 — MySQL
============================

Used by MySQL database servers.

Cybersecurity relevance:
Exposed database ports may allow attackers to access sensitive data.

============================
Port 3389 — RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol)
============================

RDP allows remote graphical access to Windows systems.

Cybersecurity relevance:
RDP is frequently targeted by attackers.

Weak passwords may lead to system compromise.

============================
Port 8080 — Alternative HTTP
============================

Often used by:

• Web applications
• Proxy servers
• Development servers

============================
What are Protocols?
============================

Protocols are rules that define how communication happens over networks.

Protocols determine:

• Data format
• Communication process
• Error handling
• Data transmission methods

Without protocols, devices would not understand each other.

============================
TCP vs UDP
============================

TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)

TCP is:

• Reliable
• Connection-oriented
• Accurate

Features:

• Error checking
• Packet sequencing
• Guaranteed delivery
• Three-way handshake

TCP Three-Way Handshake:

1. SYN
2. SYN-ACK
3. ACK

Used in:

• Websites
• Emails
• Banking systems
• File transfers

Importance:
TCP ensures no data is lost.

============================
UDP (User Datagram Protocol)
============================

UDP is:

• Faster
• Connectionless
• Less reliable

Features:

• No handshake
• No delivery guarantee
• Lower latency

Used in:

• Gaming
• Streaming
• VoIP calls
• DNS queries

Importance:
UDP is preferred where speed matters more than reliability.

============================
Common Protocols
============================

FTP (File Transfer Protocol)
Used for file transfers.

Problem:
No encryption.

SSH (Secure Shell)
Used for secure remote login.

SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol)
Used for sending emails.

SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol)
Used for network monitoring and device management.

HTTP/HTTPS
Used for web communication.

DNS
Used for domain name resolution.

SMB
Used for Windows file sharing.

============================
Dangerous Open Ports
============================

Some ports are considered dangerous when exposed publicly.

Examples:

1. Telnet (23)
No encryption.

2. FTP (21)
Plain-text credentials.

3. RDP (3389)
Frequently brute-forced by attackers.

4. SMB (445)
Often targeted by ransomware.

Cybersecurity professionals monitor open ports carefully.

============================
Port Scanning
============================

Port scanning is the process of identifying open ports and services on a target system.

Attackers use port scanning to:

• Discover services
• Identify vulnerabilities
• Plan attacks

Defenders use port scanning to:

• Audit systems
• Detect unnecessary services
• Reduce attack surface

Popular port scanning tool:

Nmap

Example:

nmap 192.168.1.1

This scans for open ports on the target.

============================
Why Open Ports Matter
============================

Every open port increases the attack surface.

If vulnerable services are exposed:

• Attackers may exploit them
• Malware may spread
• Unauthorized access may occur

Best practice:

• Close unused ports
• Use firewalls
• Keep services updated
• Use strong authentication

============================
Setting Up Your Cybersecurity Lab
============================

Cybersecurity professionals need safe environments for practicing.

Instead of attacking real systems, students create virtual labs.

A virtual lab allows:

• Ethical hacking practice
• Malware analysis
• Networking experiments
• Vulnerability testing

Safely and legally.

============================
VirtualBox
============================

VirtualBox is a virtualization software that allows multiple operating systems to run on one computer.

Example:

• Windows host system
• Kali Linux virtual machine

Benefits:

• Safe testing environment
• Easy snapshots and recovery
• Isolation from main system

============================
Kali Linux
============================

Kali Linux is a cybersecurity-focused Linux distribution used for:

• Penetration testing
• Ethical hacking
• Digital forensics
• Security research

Kali contains hundreds of cybersecurity tools.

Examples:

• Nmap
• Wireshark
• Burp Suite
• Metasploit
• Aircrack-ng

============================
Basic Lab Setup Process
============================

1. Install VirtualBox.
2. Download Kali Linux ISO.
3. Create a virtual machine.
4. Allocate RAM and storage.
5. Install Kali Linux.
6. Boot the VM.
7. Test networking.

Recommended minimum:

• 2 GB RAM
• 20 GB storage

============================
Important Linux Networking Commands
============================

ifconfig:
Displays network interface information.

ip a:
Displays IP addresses and interfaces.

whoami:
Shows current logged-in user.

netstat -tuln:
Shows listening ports and active services.

ping:
Tests network connectivity.

============================
Why Labs are Important in Cybersecurity
============================

Cybersecurity is highly practical.

Labs help students:

• Understand networking better
• Learn ethical hacking safely
• Practice scanning and enumeration
• Analyze vulnerabilities
• Build real-world skills

A proper lab setup is the foundation for learning penetration testing and advanced cybersecurity topics.
keyPoints: Ports are logical communication endpoints
Protocols define communication rules between systems
Well-known ports range from 0–1023
TCP provides reliable communication while UDP focuses on speed
Common ports include 21, 22, 53, 80, 443, and 3389
Dangerous open ports may expose systems to attacks
Port scanning identifies running services and open ports
Nmap is one of the most popular port scanning tools
VirtualBox allows safe cybersecurity practice using virtual machines
Kali Linux contains many penetration testing tools
Open ports increase the attack surface of systems
Cybersecurity labs are essential for practical learning

days.title: Linux Basics & Virtual Machines Deep Dive

content.theory:
Before learning ethical hacking and cybersecurity tools, it is extremely important to understand Linux and Virtual Machines. Most cybersecurity professionals use Linux because it provides powerful networking capabilities, security tools, customization, automation, and better control over systems.

Cybersecurity is heavily command-line based, and Linux is the preferred operating system for penetration testers, SOC analysts, security researchers, malware analysts, and ethical hackers.

============================
What is an Operating System?
============================

An Operating System (OS) is system software that acts as an interface between users and computer hardware.

The operating system manages:

• Memory
• CPU
• Storage
• Files
• Devices
• Applications
• Networking

Without an operating system, users cannot interact with computers properly.

Examples of operating systems:

• Windows
• Linux
• macOS
• Android
• iOS

The operating system is responsible for:

• Running applications
• Managing files
• Handling processes
• Managing hardware communication
• Providing security controls

For example:

When a user opens a browser:

• The OS allocates memory
• The CPU processes instructions
• Files are loaded from storage
• Network communication begins

The operating system coordinates all these activities.

============================
Types of Operating Systems
============================

1. Desktop Operating Systems
Used on personal computers.

Examples:

• Windows
• Linux
• macOS

2. Server Operating Systems
Designed for servers and enterprise systems.

Examples:

• Ubuntu Server
• Windows Server
• Red Hat Enterprise Linux

3. Mobile Operating Systems
Used on smartphones and tablets.

Examples:

• Android
• iOS

4. Embedded Operating Systems
Used in devices like routers, IoT devices, smart TVs, and industrial systems.

============================
What is Linux?
============================

Linux is an open-source operating system based on Unix.

It was created by Linus Torvalds in 1991.

Linux is widely used because it is:

• Free and open source
• Stable
• Secure
• Lightweight
• Highly customizable
• Powerful for networking and development

Linux powers:

• Servers
• Cloud infrastructure
• Supercomputers
• Android devices
• Security systems
• Web hosting platforms

Most internet servers today run Linux.

============================
Why Linux is Preferred in Cybersecurity
============================

Cybersecurity professionals prefer Linux because:

1. Powerful Command Line
Linux provides strong command-line tools useful for networking, scripting, and automation.

2. Better Security Control
Linux allows deep control over:

• Permissions
• Processes
• Networking
• Services

3. Open Source
Security researchers can inspect source code.

4. Pre-installed Security Tools
Many Linux distributions contain penetration testing tools.

5. Stability
Linux systems are stable and efficient.

6. Lightweight Performance
Linux works well even on low-resource systems.

7. Automation and Scripting
Linux supports Bash scripting for automation.

8. Most Servers Use Linux
Learning Linux helps cybersecurity professionals secure real-world systems.

============================
Linux Distributions
============================

Different versions of Linux are called distributions (distros).

Popular Linux distributions:

• Ubuntu
• Debian
• Kali Linux
• Fedora
• Arch Linux
• Red Hat

============================
What is Kali Linux?
============================

Kali Linux is a Debian-based Linux distribution developed for cybersecurity and penetration testing.

Kali Linux comes pre-installed with hundreds of security tools.

Examples:

• Nmap
• Wireshark
• Burp Suite
• Metasploit
• Aircrack-ng
• Hydra

Kali is widely used by:

• Ethical hackers
• Penetration testers
• Security researchers
• SOC analysts

============================
What is a Virtual Machine?
============================

A Virtual Machine (VM) is a virtual computer running inside another computer.

Using virtualization software, users can run multiple operating systems on one physical machine.

Example:

Host OS:
Windows

Guest OS:
Kali Linux VM

The Kali Linux system runs inside a virtual environment.

============================
Why Virtual Machines are Important in Cybersecurity
============================

Cybersecurity professionals use virtual machines because they provide:

1. Isolation
Activities inside the VM remain separated from the host system.

2. Safe Testing
Malware and hacking tools can be tested safely.

3. Snapshots
Users can restore systems if something breaks.

4. Multiple Operating Systems
Different systems can run simultaneously.

5. Easy Lab Creation
Students can build cybersecurity labs safely.

============================
VirtualBox
============================

VirtualBox is virtualization software used to create and run virtual machines.

Features:

• Free and open-source
• Cross-platform support
• Snapshot support
• Easy VM management

Cybersecurity students commonly use VirtualBox for running Kali Linux.

============================
Understanding the Linux Filesystem
============================

Linux uses a hierarchical filesystem structure.

Everything starts from the root directory:

/

Important Linux directories:

1. / (Root)
The top-level directory.

2. /home
Stores user files and personal directories.

Example:
/home/pravesh

3. /etc
Stores configuration files.

Examples:

• Password configurations
• Network configurations
• Service settings

4. /var
Stores variable data.

Examples:

• Logs
• Cache
• Mail data

5. /bin
Contains essential system binaries and commands.

6. /tmp
Stores temporary files.

Cybersecurity relevance:
Attackers often store malicious temporary files here.

============================
Linux Command Line Interface (CLI)
============================

Linux can be controlled using:

• GUI (Graphical User Interface)
• CLI (Command Line Interface)

Cybersecurity professionals mostly use the command line because it is:

• Faster
• More powerful
• Better for automation
• Essential for remote access

============================
Basic Linux Commands
============================

ls
Lists files and directories.

Example:
ls

ls -la
Shows hidden files and detailed permissions.

============================
cd
============================

Changes directory.

Example:
cd /home

============================
pwd
============================

Displays current directory.

Example:
pwd

============================
mkdir
============================

Creates directories.

Example:
mkdir cyberlab

============================
touch
============================

Creates empty files.

Example:
touch notes.txt

============================
cat
============================

Displays file contents.

Example:
cat notes.txt

============================
grep
============================

Searches for patterns inside files.

Example:
grep password file.txt

Cybersecurity relevance:
Used heavily in log analysis.

============================
find
============================

Searches for files and directories.

Example:
find / -name test.txt

============================
chmod
============================

Changes file permissions.

Example:
chmod 755 script.sh

============================
chown
============================

Changes file ownership.

============================
ps
============================

Displays running processes.

Example:
ps aux

============================
kill
============================

Terminates processes.

Example:
kill PID

============================
top
============================

Shows real-time system processes.

============================
wget
============================

Downloads files from the internet.

============================
curl
============================

Transfers data using URLs.

Widely used in APIs and penetration testing.

============================
Linux File Permissions
============================

Linux uses permission-based security.

Permission types:

r → Read = 4
w → Write = 2
x → Execute = 1

Permissions are assigned to:

• Owner
• Group
• Others

Example:
chmod 755 file.sh

Meaning:

Owner:
Read + Write + Execute

Group:
Read + Execute

Others:
Read + Execute

============================
Why Permissions are Important
============================

Permissions help:

• Prevent unauthorized access
• Protect sensitive files
• Secure system resources

Improper permissions can lead to privilege escalation vulnerabilities.

============================
User Management in Linux
============================

Linux supports multiple users.

Important commands:

adduser:
Creates users.

passwd:
Changes passwords.

su:
Switches users.

sudo:
Runs commands with administrative privileges.

============================
Root User
============================

The root user is the administrator account.

Root has complete system control.

Cybersecurity relevance:
Attackers often attempt privilege escalation to gain root access.

============================
Package Management in Kali/Debian
============================

Kali Linux uses the APT package manager.

Important commands:

apt update:
Updates package lists.

apt upgrade:
Upgrades installed packages.

apt install:
Installs new software.

Example:
apt install nmap

============================
Text Editors in Linux
============================

nano:
Beginner-friendly terminal editor.

vim:
Advanced and powerful editor.

gedit:
GUI-based text editor.

Cybersecurity professionals frequently edit:

• Scripts
• Configurations
• Logs
• Payloads

============================
Piping and Redirection
============================

Linux allows command chaining.

Pipe (|):
Sends output of one command into another.

Example:
cat file.txt | grep password

Overwrite (> ):
Writes output into a file.

Append (>>):
Adds output to a file.

Input Redirection (<):
Reads input from files.

============================
Networking Commands in Linux
============================

ping:
Tests connectivity.

ifconfig:
Displays network interfaces.

ip a:
Shows IP addresses.

netstat:
Displays network connections.

ss:
Shows socket statistics.

nmap:
Performs port scanning.

curl:
Sends web requests.

wget:
Downloads files.

============================
Process Management
============================

Linux treats running programs as processes.

Important commands:

ps aux:
Lists running processes.

kill PID:
Terminates processes.

killall:
Kills processes by name.

bg:
Runs process in background.

fg:
Brings process to foreground.

jobs:
Shows background jobs.

============================
VM Snapshots
============================

Snapshots save the current state of a VM.

Benefits:

• Rollback after mistakes
• Malware recovery
• Safe experimentation

Cybersecurity students should take snapshots before testing tools or malware.

============================
Why Linux Skills are Important in Cybersecurity
============================

Linux is the backbone of cybersecurity.

Most security tools and servers run on Linux.

Strong Linux knowledge helps in:

• Ethical Hacking
• Penetration Testing
• SOC Operations
• Malware Analysis
• Threat Hunting
• Cloud Security
• Digital Forensics

Cybersecurity professionals spend a large amount of time using Linux terminals, networking tools, scripts, and system utilities.

Mastering Linux basics is one of the most important steps toward becoming a successful cybersecurity professional.
keyPoints: An operating system manages hardware and software resources
Linux is widely used in cybersecurity because it is secure and customizable
Kali Linux is a penetration testing-focused Linux distribution
Virtual machines provide safe environments for cybersecurity practice
Linux uses a hierarchical filesystem structure
The command line is essential for cybersecurity professionals
Linux permissions protect files and system resources
APT is the package manager used in Kali Linux
Commands like ls, cd, grep, and find are heavily used in cybersecurity
Networking commands help analyze systems and connectivity
Process management is important for monitoring system activity
VM snapshots allow safe rollback during testing
resources: Linux Command Line by William Shotts
Hack The Box Academy Linux Basics

content.theory:
Modern websites and web applications are not just simple web pages. They are complex systems made of multiple layers working together to deliver content, process data, authenticate users, and store information.

Whenever users:

• Open websites
• Log into applications
• Shop online
• Watch videos
• Use social media
• Send messages

A complete web application architecture works behind the scenes.

Understanding web application architecture is extremely important in cybersecurity because most modern cyber attacks target web applications.

Examples:

• SQL Injection
• Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
• Authentication bypass
• Session hijacking
• API abuse
• CSRF attacks

Before learning web hacking techniques, it is necessary to understand how web applications work internally.

============================
What is a Web Application?
============================

A web application is software that runs through a web browser.

Examples:

• Facebook
• Instagram
• Amazon
• Gmail
• YouTube
• Banking applications

Unlike traditional desktop applications, web applications communicate using the internet.

Users interact with web applications using browsers such as:

• Chrome
• Firefox
• Edge
• Safari

============================
How Web Applications Work
============================

Suppose a user opens a website.

The following process occurs:

1. The browser sends an HTTP request.
2. The request reaches the web server.
3. The server processes the request.
4. The server communicates with databases or APIs.
5. The server generates a response.
6. The response is sent back to the browser.
7. The browser displays the webpage.

This process is called the HTTP Request/Response Cycle.

============================
Client-Side vs Server-Side
============================

Web applications are divided into two major parts:

1. Client-Side
2. Server-Side

============================
Client-Side
============================

The client-side refers to everything running inside the user’s browser.

Technologies used:

• HTML
• CSS
• JavaScript

Responsibilities:

• Displaying webpages
• User interaction
• Form validation
• Dynamic page updates
• UI/UX handling

Users can view and modify client-side code.

Example:

• Right-click → Inspect Element
• Viewing page source

Cybersecurity relevance:

Attackers may manipulate:

• HTML forms
• JavaScript code
• Client-side validations
• Cookies

Important:
Client-side security alone is never enough because users control the browser.

============================
HTML (HyperText Markup Language)
============================

HTML structures web pages.

Example:

• Headings
• Paragraphs
• Forms
• Buttons
• Images

HTML is the backbone of webpage structure.

============================
CSS (Cascading Style Sheets)
============================

CSS controls webpage appearance.

Examples:

• Colors
• Fonts
• Layouts
• Animations

============================
JavaScript
============================

JavaScript adds interactivity to webpages.

Examples:

• Login validation
• Dynamic updates
• Popups
• API requests
• Animations

Cybersecurity relevance:

JavaScript vulnerabilities may lead to:

• XSS attacks
• DOM manipulation
• Token leakage

============================
Server-Side
============================

The server-side refers to code running on the web server.

Users cannot directly see server-side code.

Responsibilities:

• Processing requests
• Authentication
• Business logic
• Database communication
• File handling
• Access control

Popular server-side languages:

• PHP
• Python
• Node.js
• Java
• C#
• Ruby

Example:

When a user logs into a website:

• The browser sends credentials.
• The server checks the database.
• The server verifies the password.
• The server creates a session.

All this happens server-side.

Cybersecurity relevance:

Many serious vulnerabilities occur on servers.

Examples:

• SQL Injection
• Authentication bypass
• Remote Code Execution (RCE)
• File upload vulnerabilities

============================
Three-Tier Architecture
============================

Most web applications use Three-Tier Architecture.

The three layers are:

1. Presentation Layer
2. Application Layer
3. Data Layer

============================
1. Presentation Layer
============================

This is the frontend visible to users.

Examples:

• Browser UI
• HTML pages
• CSS
• JavaScript

============================
2. Application Layer
============================

This layer contains backend logic.

Responsibilities:

• Authentication
• Request processing
• Business logic
• API handling

============================
3. Data Layer
============================

This layer stores data.

Examples:

• MySQL
• PostgreSQL
• MongoDB
• Oracle Database

Cybersecurity relevance:

Databases are common attack targets.

Example:
SQL Injection attacks target databases.

============================
HTTP Request/Response Cycle
============================

Web communication works using HTTP requests and responses.

Step-by-step:

1. Browser sends request.
2. Server receives request.
3. Server processes request.
4. Server sends response.
5. Browser renders content.

Example:

User requests:

GET /login HTTP/1.1

Server responds:

HTTP/1.1 200 OK

============================
URL Structure
============================

A URL (Uniform Resource Locator) identifies resources on the internet.

Example:

https://example.com:443/login?user=test#section

Breakdown:

https:// → Protocol
example.com → Domain
443 → Port
/login → Path
?user=test → Query parameter
#section → Fragment

Cybersecurity relevance:

Attackers often manipulate:

• Query parameters
• Paths
• URLs

============================
HTTP Methods
============================

HTTP methods define actions.

GET:
Retrieves data.

POST:
Sends data.

PUT:
Updates resources.

DELETE:
Deletes resources.

PATCH:
Partially updates resources.

Cybersecurity relevance:

Improperly secured methods may create vulnerabilities.

============================
HTTP Status Codes
============================

Servers return status codes.

Common codes:

200 — OK
Request successful.

301 — Redirect
Resource moved.

400 — Bad Request
Invalid request.

401 — Unauthorized
Authentication required.

403 — Forbidden
Access denied.

404 — Not Found
Resource missing.

500 — Internal Server Error
Server-side issue.

Cybersecurity relevance:

Status codes reveal application behavior.

============================
Request Headers
============================

Headers provide additional request information.

Common request headers:

Host:
Target domain.

User-Agent:
Browser or client information.

Accept:
Accepted content types.

Authorization:
Authentication tokens.

Cookie:
Session information.

Content-Type:
Defines request data type.

Cybersecurity relevance:

Attackers often manipulate headers.

============================
Response Headers
============================

Servers send response headers.

Important response headers:

Content-Type:
Defines response format.

Set-Cookie:
Sets session cookies.

Location:
Used for redirects.

X-Frame-Options:
Protects against clickjacking.

Content-Security-Policy (CSP):
Helps prevent XSS attacks.

Strict-Transport-Security:
Forces HTTPS.

Cybersecurity relevance:

Missing security headers increase risk.

============================
Cookies and Sessions
============================

Cookies store small pieces of data inside browsers.

Used for:

• Authentication
• Session tracking
• Preferences

Sessions help servers identify logged-in users.

Cybersecurity relevance:

Session hijacking attacks steal session cookies.

============================
REST API
============================

REST (Representational State Transfer) is a popular API architecture.

REST APIs use HTTP methods for communication.

Examples:

GET /users
POST /login
DELETE /account

REST APIs are stateless.

Meaning:
Each request contains all necessary information.

Modern applications heavily rely on APIs.

Cybersecurity relevance:

API vulnerabilities include:

• Broken authentication
• Rate-limit bypass
• Insecure object references
• Token leakage

============================
Same-Origin Policy (SOP)
============================

The Same-Origin Policy is a browser security mechanism.

It prevents websites from accessing data belonging to other websites.

Example:

A malicious website should not read data from your banking website.

SOP protects:

• Cookies
• Sessions
• Sensitive browser data

Without SOP, websites could steal user data easily.

============================
Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS)
============================

Sometimes websites need cross-origin communication.

CORS allows controlled sharing between different origins.

Example:

Frontend:
app.example.com

API:
api.example.com

Cybersecurity relevance:

Misconfigured CORS can expose sensitive data.

============================
Burp Suite
============================

Burp Suite is one of the most important web security testing tools.

Used for:

• Intercepting requests
• Modifying traffic
• Analyzing headers
• Testing vulnerabilities

Burp Suite acts as a proxy between browsers and servers.

Cybersecurity professionals use Burp Suite extensively during penetration testing.

============================
Proxy Concept
============================

A proxy sits between a client and server.

Browser → Proxy → Server

The proxy can:

• Capture traffic
• Modify requests
• Analyze responses

Burp Suite commonly uses:

127.0.0.1:8080

============================
Why Web Architecture is Important in Cybersecurity
============================

Most modern attacks target web applications.

Understanding architecture helps security professionals:

• Identify vulnerabilities
• Analyze traffic
• Test APIs
• Secure authentication systems
• Understand attack surfaces

Web application knowledge is essential for:

• Ethical Hacking
• Web Penetration Testing
• Bug Bounty Hunting
• API Security Testing
• SOC Operations
• Threat Hunting

Before learning advanced web attacks, students must first understand how browsers, servers, APIs, requests, responses, sessions, and databases work together.
keyPoints: Web applications contain client-side and server-side components
HTML, CSS, and JavaScript run in the browser
Server-side code processes requests and interacts with databases
Most web applications use three-tier architecture
HTTP communication works using requests and responses
URLs contain protocol, domain, port, path, and query parameters
HTTP methods define actions such as GET and POST
Status codes help identify server responses
Headers contain important request and response information
REST APIs use stateless HTTP communication
Same-Origin Policy protects browser data
Burp Suite is widely used for web security testing
Most modern cyber attacks target web applications
resources: Hack The Box Academy Web Requests Module

content.theory:
Modern web applications need a way to identify users and remember their activity. For example, when a user logs into Facebook, Gmail, or Amazon, the website remembers the user even when multiple pages are opened.

However, HTTP itself is a stateless protocol.

This means:

• Every HTTP request is independent
• The server does not automatically remember previous requests
• The server does not inherently know whether a user is logged in

To solve this problem, web applications use:

• Cookies
• Sessions
• Authentication mechanisms

These technologies help websites identify users, maintain login states, manage permissions, and secure communication.

Understanding cookies, sessions, and authentication is extremely important in cybersecurity because many web vulnerabilities target these mechanisms.

Examples:

• Session hijacking
• Cookie theft
• Authentication bypass
• CSRF attacks
• Weak password attacks
• Token theft

============================
What are Cookies?
============================

Cookies are small pieces of data stored inside the user’s browser.

Web servers send cookies to browsers, and browsers automatically send them back with future requests.

Cookies help websites remember:

• Logged-in users
• User preferences
• Shopping cart items
• Session IDs
• Tracking information

Without cookies, users would need to log in repeatedly on every page.

============================
How Cookies Work
============================

Step-by-step process:

1. User logs into a website.
2. The server creates a cookie.
3. The browser stores the cookie.
4. Future requests automatically include the cookie.
5. The server identifies the user using the cookie.

Example:

Server sends:

Set-Cookie: sessionid=abc123

Browser stores it.

Future requests:

Cookie: sessionid=abc123

The server now knows which user is making the request.

============================
Where Cookies are Stored
============================

Cookies are stored inside browsers.

Different browsers manage cookies separately.

Examples:

• Chrome cookies
• Firefox cookies
• Edge cookies

Users can:

• View cookies
• Delete cookies
• Modify cookies

Using browser developer tools.

============================
Types of Cookies
============================

1. Session Cookies
Temporary cookies deleted when the browser closes.

Used for:

• Login sessions
• Temporary authentication

2. Persistent Cookies
Remain stored until expiration.

Used for:

• Remember me features
• User preferences
• Tracking

3. Third-Party Cookies
Created by external services.

Often used for:

• Advertisements
• Analytics
• Tracking users across websites

============================
Cookie Attributes
============================

Cookies contain attributes that improve security.

============================
HttpOnly
============================

Prevents JavaScript from accessing cookies.

Importance:

Protects against cookie theft through XSS attacks.

Without HttpOnly:

Malicious JavaScript may steal session cookies.

============================
Secure
============================

Allows cookies to be sent only over HTTPS.

Importance:

Prevents cookies from being exposed over insecure HTTP.

============================
SameSite
============================

Controls cross-site cookie behavior.

Used to protect against:

• CSRF attacks

Types:

• Strict
• Lax
• None

============================
Domain
============================

Defines which domain can access the cookie.

============================
Path
============================

Defines which paths can access the cookie.

============================
Expires / Max-Age
============================

Defines cookie expiration time.

============================
What are Sessions?
============================

Sessions allow servers to remember users between requests.

When a user logs in:

1. The server creates a session.
2. A session ID is generated.
3. The session ID is stored in a cookie.
4. Future requests include the session ID.
5. The server identifies the user using the session.

The actual session data usually remains stored on the server.

============================
Session ID
============================

A session ID is a unique identifier assigned to users.

Example:

sessionid=XYZ123ABC

This ID acts like a temporary identity card.

If attackers steal the session ID, they may impersonate users.

============================
Why Sessions are Important
============================

Sessions help maintain:

• Login state
• Authentication
• User activity
• Shopping carts
• User-specific data

Without sessions, users would constantly need to authenticate repeatedly.

============================
Session Management
============================

Good session management is critical for web security.

Secure practices include:

• Random session IDs
• Secure cookies
• Session expiration
• Session regeneration after login
• HTTPS-only communication

Weak session management leads to vulnerabilities.

============================
Session Fixation
============================

Session fixation occurs when attackers force victims to use a known session ID.

Example:

1. Attacker creates a session.
2. Attacker sends the session ID to victim.
3. Victim logs in using that session.
4. Attacker reuses the same session ID.

Result:
Attacker hijacks the session.

============================
Session Hijacking
============================

Session hijacking occurs when attackers steal valid session cookies.

Methods include:

• XSS attacks
• Packet sniffing
• Malware
• Browser compromise

Once attackers obtain session cookies, passwords may not even be needed.

============================
Authentication
============================

Authentication is the process of verifying identity.

Example:

• Logging into Gmail
• Banking login
• Social media login

Authentication answers:

“Who are you?”

============================
Authorization
============================

Authorization determines what authenticated users are allowed to do.

Example:

• Normal user
• Admin user
• Moderator

Authorization answers:

“What are you allowed to access?”

============================
Authentication vs Authorization
============================

Authentication:
Verifies identity.

Authorization:
Controls permissions.

Example:

A user may successfully log in (authentication) but still not access admin panels (authorization).

============================
Types of Authentication
============================

1. Something You Know

Examples:

• Passwords
• PINs

2. Something You Have

Examples:

• OTP tokens
• Mobile devices
• Smart cards

3. Something You Are

Examples:

• Fingerprints
• Face recognition
• Iris scans

Using multiple methods together creates Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA).

============================
Why MFA is Important
============================

Even if attackers steal passwords, they still need the second factor.

MFA greatly improves security.

============================
Password Storage
============================

Passwords should NEVER be stored in plain text.

If databases are leaked, attackers could immediately read passwords.

Secure applications use hashing.

============================
Hashing
============================

Hashing converts passwords into irreversible values.

Popular password hashing algorithms:

• bcrypt
• scrypt
• Argon2

Good password hashing includes:

• Salting
• Strong algorithms
• Slow computation

============================
Why MD5 and SHA1 are Weak
============================

Older algorithms like:

• MD5
• SHA1

Are considered insecure for password storage.

Attackers can crack them using:

• Rainbow tables
• GPU cracking
• Brute force attacks

============================
Common Authentication Vulnerabilities
============================

1. Weak Passwords
Easy-to-guess passwords.

2. Default Credentials
Using default usernames and passwords.

3. No Account Lockout
Unlimited login attempts enable brute-force attacks.

4. Plain Text Transmission
Passwords sent without HTTPS.

5. Weak Session Management
Predictable or insecure session IDs.

6. Password Reuse
Users reusing passwords across websites.

============================
JWT (JSON Web Token)
============================

JWT is a token-based authentication system widely used in APIs.

JWT Structure:

header.payload.signature

Example:

xxxxx.yyyyy.zzzzz

============================
JWT Components
============================

1. Header
Contains algorithm information.

2. Payload
Contains user data and claims.

3. Signature
Verifies token integrity.

============================
Advantages of JWT
============================

• Stateless authentication
• Scalable systems
• API-friendly
• Easy integration

============================
JWT Risks
============================

Improper JWT handling may lead to:

• Token theft
• Weak signing secrets
• Token tampering
• Sensitive data exposure

============================
OAuth 2.0
============================

OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework.

It allows third-party applications to access resources without sharing passwords.

Example:

“Login with Google”

Instead of sharing passwords with websites, users authorize access through Google.

============================
OAuth Components
============================

1. Resource Owner
The user.

2. Client Application
