weeks.miniProject: Secure Login Page or Basic Vulnerability Scanner

content.theory:
Cybersecurity is not only about attacking or testing systems. One of the most important parts of security is building applications securely from the beginning.

Most vulnerabilities exist because developers write insecure code, trust user input, expose sensitive information, or fail to consider how attackers might abuse the application.

Secure Coding is the practice of writing software in a way that reduces vulnerabilities and protects applications from attacks.

Secure coding helps developers:

• Prevent vulnerabilities
• Protect sensitive data
• Reduce attack surfaces
• Build secure systems
• Improve software reliability

Understanding secure coding is important for:

• Developers
• Ethical Hackers
• Penetration Testers
• Security Engineers
• DevSecOps Teams
• SOC Analysts

Security professionals must understand both:

• How vulnerabilities happen
• How to prevent them

============================
Why Secure Coding Matters
============================

Modern applications handle:

• User credentials
• Payment information
• Medical data
• Personal records
• Cloud infrastructure
• APIs

Insecure code may lead to:

• Data breaches
• Account takeover
• Financial loss
• Remote code execution
• Service disruption

Many real-world attacks happen because developers fail to implement secure coding practices.

============================
How Vulnerabilities Happen
============================

Applications constantly accept input from users.

Examples:

• Login forms
• Search boxes
• File uploads
• APIs
• Comments
• URL parameters

If applications trust user input without validation, attackers may inject malicious payloads.

Examples:

• SQL Injection
• XSS
• Command Injection
• File Inclusion

Attackers think differently from developers.

Developers often think:

“How should users use this feature?”

Attackers think:

“How can this feature be abused?”

============================
Core Principles of Secure Coding
============================

Secure coding follows several important principles.

============================
1. Never Trust User Input
============================

User input should always be considered untrusted.

Examples of user-controlled input:

• Form fields
• Cookies
• HTTP headers
• File uploads
• URL parameters
• API requests

Attackers may manipulate any input.

Applications must:

• Validate input
• Sanitize input
• Restrict dangerous characters

============================
Input Validation
============================

Input validation checks whether data is:

• Correct
• Expected
• Safe

Examples:

• Email format validation
• Numeric-only fields
• Length restrictions
• File type restrictions

============================
Client-Side Validation
============================

Performed inside browsers using JavaScript.

Example:

Checking password length before submission.

Important:

Client-side validation alone is NOT secure.

Attackers can bypass browser controls.

============================
Server-Side Validation
============================

Validation performed on the server.

This is mandatory because attackers fully control client-side requests.

Secure applications always validate input server-side.

============================
Whitelisting vs Blacklisting
============================

============================
Whitelisting
============================

Allows only approved input.

Example:

Allow only digits.

Whitelisting is safer.

============================
Blacklisting
============================

Blocks known dangerous input.

Example:

Blocking <script>

Blacklisting is weaker because attackers constantly create bypasses.

============================
Parameterized Queries (Prepared Statements)
============================

One of the most important secure coding practices is using parameterized queries.

Prepared statements separate:

• SQL code
• User data

This prevents SQL Injection.

============================
Example of Vulnerable SQL Code
============================

$query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE user='" . $_GET['user'] . "'";

Problem:

User input is directly inserted into the query.

Attackers may inject malicious SQL.

============================
Example SQL Injection Payload
============================

' OR '1'='1

The query becomes manipulated.

============================
Secure Prepared Statement Example
============================

$stmt = $pdo->prepare('SELECT * FROM users WHERE user = ?');
$stmt->execute([$_GET['user']]);

Why this is secure:

• User input is treated as data
• SQL structure remains unchanged
• Injection becomes difficult

Prepared statements are one of the best defenses against SQL Injection.

============================
Output Encoding
============================

Output encoding converts dangerous characters into safe representations before displaying them in webpages.

This helps prevent XSS attacks.

Example:

< becomes <

> becomes >

============================
Why Output Encoding is Important
============================

Suppose a user submits:

<script>alert('XSS')</script>

Without encoding:

The browser executes JavaScript.

With encoding:

The browser displays harmless text.

============================
Context-Based Encoding
============================

Different contexts require different encoding.

Examples:

• HTML encoding
• JavaScript encoding
• URL encoding
• CSS encoding

Incorrect encoding may still allow XSS.

============================
Principle of Least Privilege
============================

The Principle of Least Privilege means:

Users, applications, and services should only have the minimum permissions required.

Examples:

• Database accounts should not have admin access unless necessary
• Applications should not run as root
• Users should not receive unnecessary privileges

============================
Why Least Privilege Matters
============================

If attackers compromise an application:

Limited privileges reduce damage.

Example:

A read-only database account cannot delete data.

============================
Secure Error Handling
============================

Applications should never expose sensitive internal errors to users.

Examples of dangerous errors:

• Stack traces
• SQL errors
• File paths
• Framework versions

Attackers use verbose errors for reconnaissance.

============================
Bad Example
============================

Database connection failed:
root password incorrect.

This exposes sensitive details.

============================
Secure Approach
============================

Show generic errors to users.

Example:

“An internal error occurred.”

Log detailed errors internally for administrators.

============================
Logging and Monitoring
============================

Applications should log:

• Authentication failures
• Suspicious activity
• Errors
• Access attempts
• Administrative actions

Proper logging helps:

• Detect attacks
• Investigate incidents
• Support forensic analysis

============================
Secure Defaults
============================

Applications should be secure by default.

Examples:

• Debug mode disabled
• HTTPS enabled
• Strong password policies
• Unused services disabled

Users should not need to manually secure applications after installation.

============================
Dependency Management
============================

Modern applications rely heavily on third-party libraries.

Examples:

• Frameworks
• JavaScript packages
• APIs
• Plugins

Outdated dependencies may contain known vulnerabilities.

============================
Why Dependency Management is Critical
============================

Many breaches occur because organizations fail to update vulnerable libraries.

Examples:

• Log4Shell
• Vulnerable WordPress plugins
• Outdated npm packages

Applications should:

• Update dependencies regularly
• Remove unused packages
• Monitor CVEs

============================
Sensitive Data Protection
============================

Sensitive information should never be exposed inside source code.

Examples:

• Passwords
• API keys
• Cloud credentials
• Tokens
• Database credentials

============================
Hardcoded Credentials
============================

Hardcoding secrets inside source code is dangerous.

Example:

$password = "admin123";

Attackers may discover secrets through:

• Source code leaks
• Git repositories
• Backups
• Reverse engineering

============================
Secure Secret Management
============================

Secrets should be stored securely using:

• Environment variables
• Secret management systems
• Vault solutions

Examples:

• AWS Secrets Manager
• HashiCorp Vault
• Azure Key Vault

============================
Authentication Security
============================

Secure coding also includes secure authentication.

Best practices:

• Strong password hashing
• MFA support
• Session expiration
• Account lockout
• Secure cookies

============================
Password Hashing
============================

Passwords should never be stored in plain text.

Recommended algorithms:

• bcrypt
• scrypt
• Argon2

Weak hashing algorithms such as MD5 and SHA1 are insecure.

============================
Secure File Uploads
============================

File uploads are dangerous if not validated.

Attackers may upload:

• Web shells
• Malware
• Executable scripts

Applications should:

• Restrict file types
• Rename uploaded files
• Scan uploads
• Store files securely

============================
Security Headers
============================

Applications should use secure HTTP headers.

Examples:

• Content-Security-Policy
• X-Frame-Options
• Strict-Transport-Security
• X-Content-Type-Options

Security headers help reduce common web attacks.

============================
Code Review
============================

Secure coding also includes reviewing code for vulnerabilities.

Code reviews help identify:

• Logic flaws
• Security weaknesses
• Unsafe functions
• Hardcoded secrets

Security-focused code review is extremely important.

============================
Static and Dynamic Analysis
============================

============================
Static Analysis
============================

Analyzes source code without executing it.

Examples:

• SonarQube
• Semgrep
• Checkmarx

============================
Dynamic Analysis
============================

Tests running applications.

Examples:

• Burp Suite
• OWASP ZAP

============================
Secure Development Lifecycle (SDLC)
============================

Security should be integrated throughout development.

Secure SDLC includes:

• Threat modeling
• Secure design
• Code review
• Security testing
• Patch management

Security should not be added only after development is complete.

============================
OWASP Secure Coding Practices
============================

OWASP provides secure coding guidelines covering:

• Authentication
• Input validation
• Session management
• Cryptography
• Access control

These guidelines are widely used in industry.

============================
Why Secure Coding is Important in Cybersecurity
============================

Most cyber attacks exploit insecure code.

Understanding secure coding helps professionals:

• Prevent vulnerabilities
• Build secure applications
• Understand exploitation techniques
• Improve code quality
• Reduce attack surfaces

Strong secure coding knowledge is essential for:

• Developers
• Security Engineers
• Ethical Hackers
• Penetration Testers
• DevSecOps Teams

Cybersecurity is not only about finding vulnerabilities — it is also about preventing them before attackers can exploit them.
keyPoints: Secure coding helps prevent vulnerabilities before deployment
Applications should never trust user input
Server-side validation is mandatory for security
Prepared statements prevent SQL Injection
Output encoding helps prevent XSS attacks
Least privilege reduces attack impact
Verbose error messages may leak sensitive information
Dependencies should be updated regularly
Secrets should never be hardcoded in source code
Passwords must be securely hashed using bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2
File uploads require strict validation
Secure coding is essential for building secure applications
resources: OWASP Secure Coding Practices Quick Reference Guide

content.theory:
Passwords are the most widely used authentication mechanism in modern systems. Almost every application, website, operating system, cloud service, and enterprise platform relies on passwords for authentication.

However, passwords are also one of the biggest security weaknesses.

Attackers constantly target passwords because compromising credentials often provides direct access to systems and sensitive data.

Understanding password security is essential for:

• Ethical Hacking
• Penetration Testing
• SOC Operations
• Security Engineering
• Identity and Access Management
• Secure Development

Modern cybersecurity heavily focuses on:

• Protecting passwords
• Secure password storage
• Detecting password attacks
• Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)

============================
Why Password Security Matters
============================

Weak password security may lead to:

• Account takeover
• Data breaches
• Financial theft
• Unauthorized access
• Identity theft
• Privilege escalation

Many real-world breaches happen because attackers successfully steal or guess passwords.

============================
Common Password Problems
============================

Users often:

• Reuse passwords
• Choose weak passwords
• Use predictable patterns
• Store passwords insecurely
• Share passwords

Examples of weak passwords:

• password123
• admin
• qwerty
• 123456

Attackers specifically target common passwords.

============================
Authentication Basics
============================

Authentication verifies identity.

Example:

Logging into an email account.

Authentication answers:

“Who are you?”

Passwords are part of:

Something you know.

============================
Password Attacks
============================

Attackers use different techniques to crack or steal passwords.

============================
1. Brute Force Attack
============================

A brute force attack tries every possible combination.

Example:

Trying:

• aaaa
• aaab
• aaac

Until the correct password is found.

Advantages for attackers:

Eventually works if no protections exist.

Disadvantages:

• Slow
• Resource intensive

Strong long passwords significantly reduce brute-force success.

============================
2. Dictionary Attack
============================

Uses lists of common passwords and words.

Examples:

• password
• welcome123
• admin123

Dictionary attacks are much faster than brute force attacks.

Attackers commonly use leaked password lists.

============================
3. Credential Stuffing
============================

Credential stuffing uses leaked username-password combinations from previous breaches.

Because many users reuse passwords, attackers attempt the same credentials on multiple websites.

Example:

A password leaked from one site may still work on:

• Gmail
• Facebook
• Banking apps

Credential stuffing is extremely common.

============================
4. Rainbow Table Attack
============================

Rainbow tables are precomputed databases of password hashes.

Instead of calculating hashes repeatedly, attackers simply look them up.

Rainbow tables are effective against:

• Unsalted hashes
• Weak hashing algorithms

Modern salting techniques reduce rainbow table effectiveness.

============================
5. Phishing Attacks
============================

Attackers trick users into revealing passwords.

Examples:

• Fake login pages
• Fake emails
• Fake OTP requests

Phishing remains one of the most successful attack methods.

============================
6. Keylogging
============================

Malware records keyboard input.

Attackers steal:

• Passwords
• OTPs
• Sensitive data

============================
Hashing vs Encryption
============================

Many beginners confuse hashing and encryption.

============================
What is Hashing?
============================

Hashing converts data into a fixed-length output.

Hashing is:

• One-way
• Irreversible

Example:

password → hash value

You should not be able to reverse the hash back into the original password.

============================
Why Passwords are Hashed
============================

Applications should never store plain-text passwords.

Instead:

1. User enters password.
2. Application hashes password.
3. Hash is stored in the database.

During login:

• Input password is hashed again.
• Hashes are compared.

============================
What is Encryption?
============================

Encryption converts data into ciphertext using a key.

Unlike hashing:

Encryption is reversible.

Authorized users with the correct key can decrypt the data.

============================
Difference Between Hashing and Encryption
============================

Hashing:

• One-way
• Used for password storage
• Cannot easily recover original data

Encryption:

• Two-way
• Used for secure communication
• Data can be decrypted

============================
Weak Hashing Algorithms
============================

Older hashing algorithms are insecure.

Examples:

• MD5
• SHA1

Problems:

• Fast cracking
• Collision attacks
• Rainbow table attacks

Attackers can crack weak hashes extremely quickly using GPUs.

============================
Modern Password Hashing Algorithms
============================

Secure password hashing algorithms include:

• bcrypt
• scrypt
• Argon2

These algorithms are intentionally slow to make cracking difficult.

============================
bcrypt
============================

bcrypt is widely used for password storage.

Features:

• Built-in salting
• Adjustable cost factor
• Slow hashing

Higher cost factors increase security.

============================
scrypt
============================

scrypt is memory-intensive.

Advantages:

• Harder for GPU cracking
• Better resistance against large-scale attacks

============================
Argon2
============================

Argon2 is considered one of the strongest modern password hashing algorithms.

It won the Password Hashing Competition.

Features:

• Memory hardness
• Parallelism support
• Strong GPU resistance

============================
What is Salting?
============================

A salt is random data added to passwords before hashing.

Example:

Password:
password123

Salt:
X9$kP2

Combined before hashing.

============================
Why Salting is Important
============================

Salting prevents:

• Rainbow table attacks
• Duplicate hash recognition

Without salting:

Two users with the same password have identical hashes.

With salting:

Hashes become unique.

============================
Password Policies
============================

Organizations implement password policies to improve security.

============================
Strong Password Characteristics
============================

Good passwords should:

• Be long
• Be unique
• Avoid predictable patterns
• Avoid reused passwords

Recommended minimum:

12+ characters.

============================
Complexity Requirements
============================

Examples:

• Uppercase letters
• Lowercase letters
• Numbers
• Symbols

However, modern guidance prefers long passphrases over excessive complexity.

============================
Password Reuse
============================

Reusing passwords is dangerous.

If one website is breached:

Attackers may reuse credentials elsewhere.

============================
Password Managers
============================

Password managers securely store passwords.

Examples:

• Bitwarden
• 1Password
• LastPass
• KeePass

Benefits:

• Unique passwords
• Long passwords
• Easy management

============================
Have I Been Pwned (HIBP)
============================

HIBP is a breach notification platform.

Users can check whether:

• Emails
• Passwords

Appear in known breaches.

Organizations may integrate HIBP APIs into password security systems.

============================
Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
============================

MFA requires multiple authentication factors.

Instead of relying only on passwords.

MFA combines:

• Something you know
• Something you have
• Something you are

============================
Why MFA is Important
============================

Even if attackers steal passwords:

They still need additional authentication factors.

MFA dramatically reduces account takeover risk.

============================
Types of MFA
============================

============================
1. TOTP (Time-Based One-Time Password)
============================

Examples:

• Google Authenticator
• Authy
• Microsoft Authenticator

Apps generate temporary 6-digit codes.

Codes expire quickly.

============================
2. SMS OTP
============================

Authentication codes sent through SMS.

Weaknesses:

• SIM swapping
• SMS interception

Better than passwords alone, but weaker than app-based MFA.

============================
3. Hardware Security Keys
============================

Examples:

• YubiKey
• Titan Key

Considered one of the strongest MFA methods.

Advantages:

• Phishing resistance
• Strong cryptographic protection

============================
4. Push Notifications
============================

Users approve login requests through mobile apps.

Examples:

• Duo
• Microsoft Authenticator push

============================
MFA Vulnerabilities
============================

Although MFA improves security, it is not perfect.

============================
SIM Swapping
============================

Attackers trick telecom providers into transferring phone numbers.

This allows interception of SMS OTPs.

============================
TOTP Seed Theft
============================

If attackers steal MFA seed values, they may generate valid codes.

============================
Real-Time Phishing
============================

Tools like Evilginx proxy login pages in real time.

Attackers capture:

• Passwords
• MFA tokens
• Session cookies

============================
Passkeys and Passwordless Authentication
============================

Modern authentication is moving toward passkeys.

Passkeys use:

• Public-key cryptography
• Biometrics
• Device-based authentication

Advantages:

• Phishing resistance
• No reusable passwords

Passwordless authentication is becoming increasingly popular.

============================
Hashcat
============================

Hashcat is a powerful password cracking tool.

Used for:

• Password auditing
• Security testing
• Hash analysis

Hashcat supports:

• GPU acceleration
• Multiple algorithms
• Dictionary attacks
• Brute-force attacks

============================
Example Hashcat Command
============================

hashcat -m 0 hash.txt rockyou.txt

Here:

• -m 0 → MD5 mode
• hash.txt → hash file
• rockyou.txt → password list

============================
RockYou Wordlist
============================

RockYou is a famous password wordlist created from leaked passwords.

It contains millions of real-world passwords.

Security professionals use it for password auditing.

============================
Why bcrypt is Harder to Crack
============================

MD5 is extremely fast.

Attackers may crack millions of MD5 hashes per second.

bcrypt is intentionally slow.

This dramatically increases attack difficulty.

============================
Why Password Security Matters in Cybersecurity
============================

Compromised credentials are one of the most common causes of breaches.

Strong password security helps protect:

• Accounts
• Systems
• Cloud infrastructure
• APIs
• Enterprise environments

Understanding password attacks and MFA is essential for:

• Ethical Hackers
• Penetration Testers
• SOC Analysts
• Identity Security Teams
• Developers
• Security Engineers

Modern cybersecurity strongly depends on strong authentication and secure credential handling.

Passwords alone are no longer enough.

Strong hashing, salting, password hygiene, and MFA together provide significantly stronger protection against account compromise.
keyPoints: Passwords remain the most common authentication mechanism
Brute force, dictionary attacks, and credential stuffing target passwords
Hashing is one-way while encryption is reversible
Salting protects against rainbow table attacks
bcrypt, scrypt, and Argon2 are recommended hashing algorithms
MD5 and SHA1 are insecure for password storage
Strong passwords should be long and unique
Password reuse increases breach risks
MFA significantly reduces account takeover risks
Hardware security keys provide strong phishing-resistant MFA
Hashcat is widely used for password auditing and cracking
Modern security increasingly adopts passwordless authentication

content.theory:
Modern applications heavily rely on APIs for communication between systems, applications, mobile apps, cloud services, and web frontends.

APIs are everywhere.

When you:

• Use a mobile app
• Log into a website
• Make online payments
• Use social media
• Book a cab
• Stream videos

APIs are usually working in the background.

API stands for:

Application Programming Interface.

APIs allow applications and systems to communicate with each other.

Because APIs often process:

• Authentication
• Payments
• Sensitive data
• User information
• Business logic

They are extremely valuable targets for attackers.

API Security is one of the fastest-growing areas in cybersecurity because modern applications increasingly expose APIs to the internet.

============================
What is an API?
============================

An API is a set of rules and endpoints that allow systems to exchange data.

Example:

A mobile application requests user information from a server using an API.

The server responds with data.

APIs act as communication bridges between:

• Frontend and backend
• Applications and databases
• Mobile apps and servers
• Cloud services
• Third-party integrations

============================
Real-World API Examples
============================

Examples:

• Login systems
• Payment gateways
• Weather applications
• Google Maps integration
• Social media sharing
• Banking applications

When you refresh Instagram feeds or load YouTube videos, APIs are usually being called in the background.

============================
Why APIs are Important
============================

Modern software architecture heavily depends on APIs.

APIs help:

• Share data
• Automate communication
• Integrate services
• Build scalable applications
• Support mobile apps

APIs are essential in:

• Cloud computing
• SaaS platforms
• Microservices
• Mobile applications
• IoT systems

============================
Why API Security Matters
============================

APIs often expose:

• Sensitive user data
• Authentication tokens
• Business logic
• Financial information
• Internal systems

If APIs are insecure, attackers may:

• Access other users’ data
• Bypass authentication
• Steal tokens
• Abuse business logic
• Perform unauthorized actions

Many modern breaches involve insecure APIs.

============================
REST APIs
============================

REST stands for:

Representational State Transfer.

REST APIs are the most common type of web APIs.

REST APIs use:

• HTTP methods
• URLs
• JSON responses

============================
HTTP Methods in APIs
============================

============================
GET
============================

Retrieves data.

Example:

GET /users/1

Fetches user information.

============================
POST
============================

Creates data.

Example:

POST /posts

Creates a new post.

============================
PUT
============================

Updates existing data.

============================
DELETE
============================

Deletes data.

============================
PATCH
============================

Partially updates data.

============================
JSON in APIs
============================

Most APIs exchange data using JSON.

JSON stands for:

JavaScript Object Notation.

Example response:

{
  "id": 1,
  "name": "John",
  "email": "john@example.com"
}

JSON is lightweight and easy to process.

============================
API Endpoints
============================

An API endpoint is a URL exposing specific functionality.

Examples:

/api/users
/api/login
/api/orders
/api/products

Each endpoint performs specific operations.

============================
API Requests and Responses
============================

APIs operate using:

1. Request
2. Response

The client sends requests.

The server processes requests and returns responses.

Responses often include:

• Status codes
• Headers
• JSON data

============================
API Authentication
============================

APIs require authentication to verify users or applications.

Common authentication methods:

• API Keys
• JWT Tokens
• OAuth 2.0
• Basic Authentication

============================
API Keys
============================

Simple secret values identifying applications.

Example:

apikey=abc123

Weakness:

If leaked, attackers may abuse APIs.

============================
Basic Authentication
============================

Uses username and password.

Credentials are Base64 encoded.

Weakness:

Unsafe without HTTPS.

============================
JWT (JSON Web Token)
============================

JWTs are widely used for stateless authentication.

A JWT contains:

• Header
• Payload
• Signature

Example:

xxxxx.yyyyy.zzzzz

JWTs often store:

• User IDs
• Roles
• Permissions

============================
Why JWT Security Matters
============================

If attackers steal JWT tokens, they may impersonate users.

Weak JWT implementations may allow:

• Token forgery
• Authentication bypass
• Privilege escalation

============================
OAuth 2.0
============================

OAuth is an authorization framework.

Allows applications to access services without sharing passwords.

Example:

“Login with Google”

OAuth uses access tokens instead of passwords.

============================
OWASP API Security Top 10
============================

OWASP maintains a list of major API security risks.

============================
1. Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA)
============================

Also known as:

IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference).

One of the most critical API vulnerabilities.

============================
What is BOLA?
============================

APIs often use object IDs.

Example:

/api/users/1

If attackers change:

/api/users/1 → /api/users/2

And access another user’s data, the API is vulnerable.

============================
Why BOLA is Dangerous
============================

Attackers may:

• Access private data
• Modify accounts
• Download records
• Escalate privileges

BOLA is extremely common in APIs.

============================
2. Broken Authentication
============================

Weak authentication mechanisms allow attackers to compromise accounts.

Examples:

• Weak tokens
• Missing MFA
• Predictable sessions

============================
3. Broken Object Property Level Authorization
============================

Attackers manipulate hidden object properties.

Example:

Changing:

"role":"user"

To:

"role":"admin"

============================
4. Unrestricted Resource Consumption
============================

APIs may lack rate limiting.

Attackers may:

• Abuse resources
• Cause denial of service
• Perform mass requests

============================
5. Broken Function Level Authorization
============================

Attackers access functions they should not access.

Example:

Regular users accessing admin APIs.

============================
IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference)
============================

IDOR occurs when applications expose internal object references.

Example:

/user/1001/invoice

Attackers modify:

/user/1002/invoice

If authorization checks are missing, attackers access unauthorized data.

============================
API Enumeration
============================

Attackers often search for undocumented endpoints.

Methods include:

• Directory bruteforcing
• JavaScript analysis
• Swagger/OpenAPI leaks
• Mobile app reverse engineering

Examples of hidden endpoints:

• /api/admin
• /api/debug
• /internal

============================
Verbose Error Messages
============================

APIs sometimes expose detailed internal errors.

Examples:

• Stack traces
• SQL errors
• Framework versions
• Internal paths

Attackers use these for reconnaissance.

============================
GraphQL APIs
============================

GraphQL is an alternative to REST.

Instead of multiple endpoints:

GraphQL uses a single endpoint.

Example:

/graphql

Clients request exactly the data they need.

============================
GraphQL Security Risks
============================

Examples:

• Introspection abuse
• Excessive queries
• Batch attacks
• Authorization issues

============================
GraphQL Introspection
============================

GraphQL introspection reveals schema details.

Attackers may enumerate:

• Queries
• Mutations
• Types
• Hidden functionality

============================
API Enumeration Tools
============================

============================
Postman
============================

GUI-based API testing platform.

Used for:

• Sending requests
• Viewing responses
• Testing APIs
• Authentication testing

============================
curl
============================

Command-line HTTP client.

Example:

curl https://api.example.com/users

Widely used in Linux environments.

============================
Burp Suite
============================

Intercepts and modifies API traffic.

Useful for:

• Token analysis
• Authorization testing
• Request manipulation

============================
Swagger/OpenAPI
============================

API documentation frameworks.

Sometimes exposed publicly.

Attackers may use exposed documentation to enumerate endpoints.

============================
Rate Limiting
============================

Rate limiting restricts request frequency.

Helps prevent:

• Brute-force attacks
• API abuse
• Resource exhaustion

Without rate limiting, attackers may automate large-scale attacks.

============================
API Security Best Practices
============================

============================
1. Strong Authentication
============================

Use:

• Secure tokens
• MFA
• Proper session handling

============================
2. Proper Authorization
============================

Verify access for every request.

============================
3. Input Validation
============================

Validate all input data.

============================
4. Rate Limiting
============================

Restrict excessive requests.

============================
5. Secure Error Handling
============================

Avoid leaking internal information.

============================
6. Logging and Monitoring
============================

Track suspicious API activity.

============================
7. HTTPS Everywhere
============================

Encrypt API traffic.

============================
JSONPlaceholder API
============================

JSONPlaceholder is a free testing API.

Security students use it for:

• API learning
• HTTP method practice
• Request testing
• JSON handling

Safe for beginner practice.

============================
Why API Security is Important in Cybersecurity
============================

Modern applications increasingly expose APIs.

Attackers heavily target APIs because they often expose:

• Sensitive data
• Business logic
• Authentication systems

API security is now one of the most critical areas in:

• Web Security
• Mobile Security
• Cloud Security
• DevSecOps
• Penetration Testing
• Bug Bounty Hunting

Understanding API security helps professionals:

• Secure applications
• Detect vulnerabilities
• Protect sensitive data
• Test APIs safely
• Prevent modern attacks

As applications continue moving toward API-driven architectures, API security knowledge becomes increasingly essential for cybersecurity professionals.
keyPoints: APIs enable communication between applications and systems
REST APIs commonly use HTTP methods and JSON
API authentication includes API keys, JWT, OAuth, and Basic Auth
BOLA/IDOR is one of the most critical API vulnerabilities
Broken authorization can expose sensitive user data
GraphQL APIs have unique security risks
Postman, curl, and Burp Suite are common API testing tools
Rate limiting helps prevent API abuse
Verbose error messages may leak internal information
API enumeration discovers hidden endpoints
Secure APIs require authentication, authorization, and validation
API security is one of the fastest-growing cybersecurity fields

content.theory: The cybersecurity industry has a massive talent shortage with over 3 million unfilled positions globally. Understanding the career paths, certifications, and skills needed helps you navigate toward your goals strategically.
keyPoints: Career Paths: Penetration Tester, SOC Analyst, Security Engineer, Bug Bounty Hunter, Forensics Analyst, Security Researcher, CISO
Entry-Level Roles: Junior SOC Analyst, Security Analyst, IT Security Specialist — typically require CompTIA Security+
Key Certifications: CompTIA Security+ (entry), CEH (intermediate), OSCP (gold standard pentesting), CISSP (management), eJPT (beginner pentesting)
OSCP (Offensive Security Certified Professional): Hands-on 24-hour exam — most respected pentesting cert
Bug Bounty Platforms: HackerOne, Bugcrowd, Intigriti, Synack — earn money finding real vulnerabilities
CTF (Capture The Flag) Competitions: Hack The Box, TryHackMe, PicoCTF — gamified practice
Building a Portfolio: Document CVEs found, CTF writeups, GitHub projects, blog posts about security research
Networking: DEFCON, Black Hat conferences, local security meetups (BSides), LinkedIn security community
Salary Ranges (India): Junior Security Analyst ₹4-8 LPA, Security Engineer ₹8-20 LPA, Pentest Lead ₹15-35 LPA, CISO ₹50 LPA+
Salary Ranges (US): Junior Analyst $55-75K, Penetration Tester $85-130K, Security Architect $130-180K
content.practicalTask: Career planning exercise: (1) Create a free TryHackMe account (tryhackme.com) and complete the 'Pre-Security' learning path intro. (2) Create a free HackTheBox account. (3) Map out a 6-month learning plan: certifications you will pursue, platforms you will practice on. (4) Create a GitHub profile and initialize a 'cybersecurity-notes' repository.

content.theory: This is your Month 1 capstone. You will be assessed on all foundational concepts covered in weeks 1-4 and complete a practical project demonstrating your skills.
keyPoints: Review: CIA Triad, OSI Model, key ports, DNS, HTTP/HTTPS
Review: OWASP Top 10, SQL Injection, XSS, CSRF basics
Review: Nmap scanning, directory busting, subdomain enumeration
Review: Secure coding principles, password security, MFA
Assessment Format: 30 MCQ questions + 1 practical lab task
MCQ Topics: Networking, CIA Triad, OWASP Top 10, tool usage, port numbers, attack types
Practical Lab: Given access to DVWA, identify and document 3 vulnerabilities with proof of exploitation
content.practicalTask: Final Project — Choose one: OPTION A: Build a Secure Login Page in PHP/Python that implements: parameterized queries (prevents SQLi), password hashing (bcrypt), CSRF token, input validation, and rate limiting. OPTION B: Build a Basic Vulnerability Scanner in Python that: takes a URL as input, checks for common headers (X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy), tests for SQL injection response differences, checks for directory listing. Deploy on your local VM and document with README.
content.assignment: Submit: (1) MCQ test answers. (2) Practical lab screenshots. (3) Final project code + README + demo video (3-5 minutes).

months.title: Intermediate Security — From Analyst to Practitioner
months.goal: Move students from basic awareness to hands-on security analyst capability.

content.theory:
SQL Injection (SQLi) is one of the most dangerous and impactful web vulnerabilities. Basic SQL injection often involves simple payloads such as:

' OR 1=1--

However, modern applications may hide errors, sanitize input, or use partial protections.

Advanced SQL Injection techniques allow attackers and penetration testers to extract data even when applications do not directly display database output.

Understanding advanced SQL injection is essential for:

• Ethical Hacking
• Penetration Testing
• Web Application Security
• Bug Bounty Hunting
• Secure Development

Advanced SQLi techniques focus on:

• Data extraction
• Blind exploitation
• Enumeration
• WAF bypassing
• Database fingerprinting

============================
Recap — What is SQL Injection?
============================

SQL Injection occurs when applications insert user input directly into SQL queries without proper sanitization.

Attackers manipulate queries to:

• Bypass authentication
• Extract data
• Modify records
• Delete information
• Execute administrative operations

Example vulnerable query:

SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '$user';

If input is not sanitized, attackers may inject SQL code.

============================
Why Advanced SQL Injection Exists
============================

Modern applications often:

• Hide errors
• Filter characters
• Use WAFs
• Restrict visible output

Simple payloads may fail.

Advanced techniques help attackers continue extracting information even from partially protected systems.

============================
Types of Advanced SQL Injection
============================

Major advanced SQLi techniques include:

• UNION-Based SQL Injection
• Error-Based SQL Injection
• Boolean-Based Blind SQL Injection
• Time-Based Blind SQL Injection
• Out-of-Band SQL Injection

============================
UNION-Based SQL Injection
============================

UNION-based SQLi uses the SQL UNION operator to combine results from multiple queries.

This allows attackers to retrieve additional data from the database.

============================
What is UNION?
============================

The UNION operator combines results from two SELECT statements.

Example:

SELECT name FROM users
UNION
SELECT product FROM products

Both queries must have:

• Same number of columns
• Compatible data types

============================
How UNION-Based SQLi Works
============================

Suppose a vulnerable query exists:

SELECT name,price FROM products WHERE id='1';

An attacker injects:

' UNION SELECT username,password FROM users--

The database combines results and may display usernames and passwords.

============================
Steps in UNION-Based SQL Injection
============================

============================
1. Determine Number of Columns
============================

Attackers first identify how many columns the query contains.

Common technique:

ORDER BY testing.

Examples:

' ORDER BY 1--
' ORDER BY 2--
' ORDER BY 3--

Eventually an error occurs.

If ORDER BY 4 triggers an error:

The query likely has 3 columns.

============================
2. Identify Visible Columns
============================

Attackers determine which columns are reflected in the webpage.

Example:

' UNION SELECT 1,2,3--

If values appear on the page, those columns are visible.

============================
3. Extract Data
============================

Once visible columns are identified, attackers retrieve database information.

Example:

' UNION SELECT username,password,NULL FROM users--

============================
Useful SQL Enumeration Functions
============================

Attackers often extract:

• Database version
• Database name
• Table names
• Column names

Examples:

version()

database()

user()

============================
Error-Based SQL Injection
============================

Error-based SQLi abuses database error messages.

Applications may display:

• SQL syntax errors
• Table names
• Database versions
• File paths

Attackers intentionally trigger errors to gather information.

============================
Example Error-Based Payload
============================

' AND extractvalue(1,concat(0x7e,database()))--

This may force MySQL to reveal database information inside errors.

============================
Why Error Messages are Dangerous
============================

Verbose errors may leak:

• Database technology
• Query structure
• Internal logic
• Table names

Applications should never expose raw SQL errors to users.

============================
Blind SQL Injection
============================

Blind SQL Injection occurs when:

• No errors are visible
• No direct query output exists

Attackers must infer information indirectly.

Blind SQLi is slower but extremely powerful.

============================
Types of Blind SQL Injection
============================

• Boolean-Based Blind SQLi
• Time-Based Blind SQLi

============================
Boolean-Based Blind SQL Injection
============================

Boolean-based SQLi asks true/false questions.

Attackers observe differences in application responses.

============================
Example Payloads
============================

True condition:

' AND 1=1--

False condition:

' AND 1=2--

If responses differ:

The application is likely vulnerable.

============================
Extracting Data with Boolean SQLi
============================

Attackers extract information character by character.

Example:

' AND SUBSTRING(database(),1,1)='a'--

If the response changes:

The first character is likely “a”.

============================
Why Blind SQLi is Powerful
============================

Even without visible output, attackers may still extract:

• Database names
• Table names
• Credentials
• Sensitive records

============================
Time-Based Blind SQL Injection
============================

Time-based SQLi uses server delays.

Attackers measure response times.

============================
Example MySQL Payload
============================

' AND IF(1=1,SLEEP(5),0)--

If the server delays 5 seconds:

The condition is true.

============================
MSSQL Time Delay Example
============================

'; WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'--

============================
How Time-Based SQLi Works
============================

Attackers ask conditional questions.

Example:

Is the first character “a”?

If true:

Delay occurs.

Attackers extract information slowly using timing behavior.

============================
Out-of-Band SQL Injection (OOB SQLi)
============================

Out-of-band SQLi exfiltrates data through external communication channels.

Examples:

• DNS requests
• HTTP requests

============================
Why OOB SQLi is Used
============================

Useful when:

• Responses are hidden
• Blind SQLi is difficult
• Firewall restrictions exist

============================
Example OOB Attack
============================

The database server sends:

DNS request → attacker.com

The attacker receives extracted data externally.

============================
Database Fingerprinting
============================

Attackers often identify the database type.

Different databases use different syntax.

============================
MySQL
============================

Comments:

--
#

Functions:

version()

============================
Microsoft SQL Server (MSSQL)
============================

Delay function:

WAITFOR DELAY

============================
PostgreSQL
============================

Uses:

pg_sleep()

============================
Oracle
============================

Uses:

FROM dual

============================
SQL Injection in Different Locations
============================

SQLi may occur in many places.

============================
GET Parameters
============================

Example:

?id=1

============================
POST Requests
============================

Login forms and APIs.

============================
Cookies
============================

Session or tracking cookies.

============================
HTTP Headers
============================

Examples:

• User-Agent
• Referer
• X-Forwarded-For

Developers sometimes trust headers unsafely.

============================
Second-Order SQL Injection
============================

Payloads are stored first and executed later.

Example:

Malicious username saved in the database.

Later reused unsafely in another query.

============================
Web Application Firewalls (WAFs)
============================

WAFs attempt to detect and block malicious payloads.

Examples:

• Cloudflare
• ModSecurity
• AWS WAF

Attackers often attempt WAF bypass techniques.

============================
Common WAF Bypass Techniques
============================

============================
Case Variation
============================

Example:

SeLeCt

============================
Inline Comments
============================

Example:

SE/**/LECT

============================
URL Encoding
============================

Example:

%27 instead of '

============================
Double Encoding
============================

Payloads encoded multiple times.

============================
Whitespace Bypass
============================

Using tabs or comments instead of spaces.

============================
SQLMap
============================

SQLMap is an automated SQL injection exploitation tool.

Features:

• Detection
• Exploitation
• Database dumping
• Enumeration
• File reading

SQLMap automates advanced SQLi techniques.

============================
Why Manual SQLi Skills Matter
============================

Automated tools are powerful, but manual understanding is essential.

Manual testing helps:

• Understand logic
• Bypass protections
• Customize payloads
• Detect hidden vulnerabilities

Professional penetration testers must understand underlying techniques.

============================
Defending Against SQL Injection
============================

Best defenses:

============================
1. Prepared Statements
============================

Separate data from SQL logic.

============================
2. Parameterized Queries
============================

Prevent query manipulation.

============================
3. Input Validation
============================

Validate and sanitize input.

============================
4. Least Privilege
============================

Database accounts should have minimal permissions.

============================
5. Secure Error Handling
============================

Hide SQL errors from users.

============================
6. WAF Protection
============================

Adds additional defensive layers.

============================
PortSwigger Labs
============================

PortSwigger Web Security Academy provides free SQLi labs.

Students can practice:

• UNION attacks
• Blind SQLi
• Time-based SQLi
• Authentication bypass

These labs are widely used in cybersecurity training.

============================
Why Advanced SQL Injection Matters in Cybersecurity
============================

SQL Injection remains one of the most dangerous web vulnerabilities.

Advanced SQLi techniques help security professionals:

• Understand real-world attacks
• Perform professional penetration testing
• Identify insecure code
• Assess database exposure
• Improve web security

Understanding advanced SQLi is essential for:

• Ethical Hackers
• Penetration Testers
• Bug Bounty Hunters
• Application Security Engineers
• Red Teams

Many major breaches have occurred because organizations failed to properly secure database queries.

Strong SQL injection knowledge helps professionals both exploit vulnerabilities ethically and build stronger defensive applications.
keyPoints: Advanced SQL Injection goes beyond simple authentication bypasses
UNION-based SQLi extracts data from other database tables
Blind SQLi infers information through application behavior
Time-based SQLi uses response delays to extract data
Out-of-band SQLi exfiltrates data externally
Database fingerprinting identifies database technologies
SQL Injection may occur in parameters, cookies, headers, and POST data
WAF bypass techniques attempt to evade detection
Verbose SQL errors may leak sensitive information
Prepared statements are the strongest defense against SQLi
Manual SQLi understanding is essential despite automation tools
SQL Injection remains one of the most critical web vulnerabilities
resources: OWASP SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
Hack The Box SQL Injection Labs

content.theory:
Authentication is one of the most important security mechanisms in modern applications. Authentication systems verify user identity and control access to accounts, services, APIs, and sensitive data.

If authentication mechanisms are weak or improperly implemented, attackers may:

• Access user accounts
• Bypass login systems
• Steal sessions
• Escalate privileges
• Take over applications

Authentication vulnerabilities are among the most dangerous web security issues because compromising authentication often gives attackers direct access to sensitive systems.

Understanding authentication vulnerabilities is essential for:

• Ethical Hackers
• Penetration Testers
• Developers
• Security Engineers
• Bug Bounty Hunters
• DevSecOps Teams

============================
What is Authentication?
============================

Authentication is the process of verifying identity.

Authentication answers:

“Who are you?”

Examples:

• Logging into Gmail
• Signing into social media
• Accessing banking applications
• Logging into APIs

Applications typically use:

• Username/password
• Tokens
• Biometrics
• Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)

============================
Authentication vs Authorization
============================

Authentication:

Verifies identity.

Authorization:

Determines what users can access.

Example:

Authentication:

User successfully logs in.

Authorization:

User can only access permitted resources.

============================
Why Authentication Security Matters
============================

Weak authentication systems may lead to:

• Account takeover
• Financial fraud
• Data breaches
• Privilege escalation
• Unauthorized access

Attackers frequently target authentication because compromising accounts often bypasses other security controls.

============================
Common Authentication Vulnerabilities
============================

Major authentication vulnerabilities include:

• Username Enumeration
• Brute Force Attacks
• Credential Stuffing
• Password Reset Flaws
• 2FA Bypass
• Session Weaknesses
• OAuth Misconfigurations

============================
Username Enumeration
============================

Username enumeration occurs when applications reveal whether usernames exist.

Example:

Wrong username:

“User does not exist.”

Wrong password:

“Incorrect password.”

Attackers can identify valid accounts.

============================
Why Username Enumeration is Dangerous
============================

Once attackers know valid usernames, they can perform:

• Brute-force attacks
• Credential stuffing
• Password reset abuse
• Social engineering

============================
Preventing Username Enumeration
============================

Applications should use generic messages.

Example:

“Invalid username or password.”

Responses should:

• Look identical
• Take similar response times

============================
Brute Force Attacks
============================

Brute force attacks systematically try passwords until the correct one is found.

Attackers automate login attempts.

============================
Types of Brute Force Attacks
============================

============================
Simple Brute Force
============================

Tries every possible combination.

============================
Dictionary Attack
============================

Uses common password lists.

============================
Password Spraying
============================

Uses one common password against many users.

Example:

Trying:

Password123

Against many accounts.

============================
Why Brute Force Attacks Work
============================

Applications may lack:

• Rate limiting
• Account lockout
• CAPTCHA
• MFA

Weak passwords also increase risk.

============================
Credential Stuffing
============================

Credential stuffing uses leaked credentials from previous breaches.

Because users often reuse passwords, attackers try leaked credentials on other websites.

Example:

A password leaked from one website may also work on:

• Gmail
• Banking apps
• Social media

Credential stuffing is extremely effective.

============================
Password Reset Vulnerabilities
============================

Password reset mechanisms are common attack targets.

Weak password reset implementations may allow attackers to reset accounts without authorization.

============================
Common Password Reset Flaws
============================

• Predictable tokens
• Token reuse
• Missing expiration
• Weak verification
• Host header injection

============================
Predictable Reset Tokens
============================

Reset tokens should be random.

Weak examples:

• Sequential numbers
• Timestamps
• User IDs

Attackers may predict valid tokens.

============================
Token Reuse
============================

Password reset tokens should expire after use.

Reusable tokens allow repeated account compromise.

============================
Host Header Injection in Password Reset
============================

Some applications use the Host header when generating password reset links.

Example request:

Host: attacker.com

If the application trusts the Host header, reset links may point to attacker-controlled domains.

Victims click malicious reset links.

Attackers capture tokens.

============================
Remember Me Token Vulnerabilities
============================

Applications often implement “Remember Me” functionality.

Users remain logged in across sessions.

Weak implementations may store predictable tokens.

Example:

Base64(username:timestamp)

Attackers may forge valid cookies.

============================
Weak Persistent Login Cookies
============================

If cookies are predictable:

Attackers may generate valid authentication tokens.

Secure implementations should:

• Use cryptographically secure randomness
• Sign tokens securely
• Expire tokens properly

============================
Session Management Issues
============================

Authentication security also depends heavily on session management.

Weak sessions may lead to:

• Session hijacking
• Session fixation
• Token theft

============================
Session Fixation
============================

Attackers force victims to use attacker-controlled session IDs.

After login:

The attacker reuses the same session.

============================
Session Hijacking
============================

Attackers steal valid session cookies.

Methods:

• XSS
• Network interception
• Malware

============================
Multi-Step Authentication Bypass
============================

Some applications use multi-step login flows.

Example:

1. Enter username/password
2. Complete 2FA
3. Access account

Weak implementations may allow attackers to skip steps.

============================
Example Bypass
============================

Directly visiting:

/dashboard

Without completing 2FA.

Applications must enforce authentication checks server-side.

============================
Multi-Factor Authentication (2FA/MFA)
============================

MFA requires multiple authentication factors.

Examples:

• Password
• OTP
• Hardware key
• Biometrics

MFA significantly improves security.

============================
2FA Bypass Vulnerabilities
============================

Poorly implemented MFA may still be bypassed.

Examples:

• Direct URL access
• Weak OTP validation
• Session mismanagement
• Backup code abuse

============================
Brute Forcing OTP Codes
============================

Weak OTP implementations may:

• Allow unlimited attempts
• Lack rate limiting

Since OTPs often contain:

• 4 digits
• 6 digits

Attackers may brute-force codes.

============================
OAuth Authentication
============================

OAuth is an authorization framework.

Examples:

• Login with Google
• Login with GitHub
• Login with Facebook

OAuth uses tokens instead of sharing passwords.

============================
OAuth Components
============================

• Client
• Authorization Server
• Resource Owner
• Access Token

============================
OAuth Misconfigurations
============================

OAuth implementations may contain vulnerabilities.

Examples:

• Open redirects
• CSRF flaws
• Token leakage
• Weak redirect URI validation

============================
Open Redirects in OAuth
============================

Applications may improperly validate:

redirect_uri

Attackers redirect victims to malicious websites.

============================
OAuth CSRF
============================

Missing state parameter validation may allow CSRF attacks.

Attackers may link victim accounts to attacker-controlled accounts.

============================
Referer Header Token Leakage
============================

Tokens may leak through:

• Referer headers
• Browser history
• Logs

Sensitive tokens should never appear in URLs.

============================
Rate Limiting
============================

Rate limiting restricts repeated login attempts.

Helps prevent:

• Brute force attacks
• OTP guessing
• Credential stuffing

============================
CAPTCHA
============================

CAPTCHA attempts to distinguish humans from bots.

Used to reduce automation.

Weak CAPTCHAs may still be bypassed.

============================
Account Lockout Policies
============================

Applications may temporarily lock accounts after repeated failures.

Advantages:

• Reduces brute-force attacks

Disadvantages:

• May enable denial-of-service attacks against users

============================
Secure Authentication Best Practices
============================

============================
1. Strong Password Policies
============================

Require:

• Long passwords
• Unique passwords
• Password breach checks

============================
2. MFA Everywhere
============================

Strong MFA significantly reduces risk.

============================
3. Secure Session Management
============================

Use:

• Secure cookies
• HttpOnly
• SameSite
• Session expiration

============================
4. Generic Error Messages
============================

Prevent username enumeration.

============================
5. Rate Limiting
============================

Restrict login attempts.

============================
6. Secure Password Reset Systems
============================

Use secure random reset tokens.

============================
7. OAuth Security
============================

Validate redirect URIs and state parameters.

============================
PortSwigger Authentication Labs
============================

PortSwigger Web Security Academy provides authentication labs covering:

• Username enumeration
• Brute force attacks
• 2FA bypass
• OAuth vulnerabilities

These labs simulate real-world vulnerabilities.

============================
Why Authentication Vulnerabilities Matter in Cybersecurity
============================

Authentication systems protect user identities and sensitive resources.

Weak authentication mechanisms may allow attackers to completely compromise applications.

Understanding authentication vulnerabilities helps professionals:

• Secure login systems
• Detect weak authentication logic
• Prevent account takeover
• Improve session security
• Protect users and organizations

Authentication security is essential in:

• Web Security
• API Security
• Cloud Security
• Mobile Security
• Identity Management
• DevSecOps

Modern cybersecurity strongly depends on secure authentication systems, proper authorization, secure sessions, and strong MFA implementations.
keyPoints: Authentication verifies user identity
Username enumeration reveals valid accounts
Brute force and credential stuffing target weak passwords
Password reset systems are common attack targets
Predictable tokens create authentication weaknesses
Weak remember-me cookies may be forgeable
Session management flaws may lead to account compromise
2FA implementations may still contain bypass vulnerabilities
OAuth misconfigurations can expose tokens and accounts
Rate limiting helps reduce automated attacks
Generic login error messages reduce information leakage
Strong MFA and secure session handling improve authentication security

content.theory:
Modern web applications and APIs commonly use JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) for authentication and authorization.

JWTs are extremely popular because they allow stateless authentication, making them ideal for:

• APIs
• Mobile applications
• Cloud services
• Single-page applications (SPA)
• Microservices

However, JWT implementations are frequently insecure.

Improper JWT validation may allow attackers to:

• Bypass authentication
• Escalate privileges
• Forge tokens
• Impersonate users
• Gain administrative access

JWT vulnerabilities are very common in modern API security testing and bug bounty hunting.

Understanding JWT security is essential for:

• Ethical Hackers
• Penetration Testers
• API Security Engineers
• Developers
• DevSecOps Teams
• Bug Bounty Hunters

============================
What is JWT?
============================

JWT stands for:

JSON Web Token.

JWT is a compact token format used for:

• Authentication
• Authorization
• Identity verification

JWTs are commonly sent:

• In HTTP headers
• In cookies
• Inside API requests

============================
Why JWTs are Popular
============================

JWTs are widely used because they are:

• Stateless
• Compact
• Portable
• Easy to use with APIs

Servers do not always need to store session data.

Instead:

The token itself contains user information.

============================
JWT Structure
============================

A JWT contains three parts:

header.payload.signature

Each section is Base64URL encoded.

Example:

xxxxx.yyyyy.zzzzz

============================
1. Header
============================

The header contains metadata.

Common fields:

• alg → Signing algorithm
• typ → Token type

Example:

{
  "alg": "HS256",
  "typ": "JWT"
}

============================
2. Payload
============================

The payload contains claims.

Claims store information about the user.

Examples:

• User ID
• Roles
• Expiration time
• Permissions

Example:

{
  "sub": "123456",
  "role": "user",
  "exp": 1710000000
}

============================
Common JWT Claims
============================

============================
sub
============================

Subject identifier.

Usually the user ID.

============================
exp
============================

Expiration timestamp.

============================
iat
============================

Issued-at timestamp.

============================
nbf
============================

Not valid before timestamp.

============================
role
============================

Authorization role.

Example:

admin

============================
3. Signature
============================

The signature verifies token integrity.

The server signs the token using:

• Secret keys
• Private keys

If attackers modify token contents:

The signature should become invalid.

============================
JWT Signing Algorithms
============================

JWTs support multiple algorithms.

Common examples:

• HS256
• RS256
• ES256

============================
HS256
============================

Uses HMAC with a shared secret.

Both signing and verification use the same secret key.

============================
RS256
============================

Uses RSA public/private key cryptography.

• Private key signs tokens
• Public key verifies tokens

More secure for distributed systems.

============================
How JWT Authentication Works
============================

Typical workflow:

1. User logs in.
2. Server generates JWT.
3. Client stores token.
4. Client sends JWT with requests.
5. Server verifies JWT.

Example Authorization header:

Authorization: Bearer <JWT>

============================
Why JWT Security Matters
============================

JWTs often control:

• Authentication
• Authorization
• API access
• Admin privileges

If JWT validation is weak:

Attackers may forge valid tokens.

============================
Common JWT Vulnerabilities
============================

============================
1. alg:none Vulnerability
============================

One of the most famous JWT vulnerabilities.

Some vulnerable libraries accept unsigned tokens.

Attackers modify the header:

{
  "alg": "none"
}

Then remove the signature.

If the server accepts the token:

Authentication bypass occurs.

============================
Why alg:none is Dangerous
============================

Attackers may:

• Forge tokens
• Become administrators
• Impersonate users

Secure implementations must never allow unsigned tokens.

============================
2. Weak Secret Keys
============================

HS256 uses shared secret keys.

Weak examples:

• secret
• password
• admin123

Attackers may brute-force weak secrets.

============================
JWT Secret Cracking
============================

Attackers use tools such as:

• Hashcat
• John the Ripper

Example Hashcat mode:

-m 16500

Used for JWT cracking.

============================
Why Weak Secrets are Dangerous
============================

If attackers discover the secret key:

They can sign arbitrary tokens.

Example:

{"role":"admin"}

============================
3. RS256 to HS256 Algorithm Confusion Attack
============================

A dangerous JWT implementation flaw.

============================
How the Attack Works
============================

Suppose:

• Server expects RS256
• Public key is publicly available

If the server incorrectly accepts HS256:

Attackers may:

1. Change alg from RS256 → HS256
2. Use the public key as HMAC secret
3. Sign arbitrary tokens

The server incorrectly validates forged tokens.

============================
Why Algorithm Confusion Happens
============================

Developers fail to:

• Restrict accepted algorithms
• Properly validate JWT headers

Secure systems must strictly enforce expected algorithms.

============================
4. JWT Payload Manipulation
============================

Attackers may modify claims.

Examples:

• role=user → role=admin
• userid=1 → userid=2

If signature validation is weak:

Privilege escalation becomes possible.

============================
5. JWK Injection
============================

JWK stands for:

JSON Web Key.

Some applications allow JWTs to specify public keys using the jwk header.

Attackers may embed attacker-controlled keys.

============================
Example Attack
============================

1. Attacker generates their own key pair.
2. Embeds malicious JWK.
3. Signs arbitrary tokens.
4. Server trusts attacker-controlled keys.

============================
6. kid Injection
============================

kid stands for:

Key ID.

Servers sometimes use kid values to identify verification keys.

Example:

{
  "kid":"123"
}

============================
Why kid Injection is Dangerous
============================

If applications unsafely process kid values:

Attackers may trigger:

• SQL Injection
• Path Traversal
• Local File Inclusion

Example:

"kid":"../../../../dev/null"

============================
JWT Expiration Issues
============================

Some applications use:

• Long-lived tokens
• No expiration

Compromised tokens remain usable for long periods.

Secure JWTs should:

• Expire quickly
• Support token rotation

============================
Token Storage Risks
============================

JWT storage matters significantly.

============================
LocalStorage
============================

Vulnerable to XSS.

============================
Cookies
============================

Safer if configured with:

• HttpOnly
• Secure
• SameSite

============================
JWT Enumeration and Analysis
============================

Attackers analyze JWTs for:

• Weak algorithms
• Sensitive claims
• Misconfigurations

JWTs are easy to decode because Base64 encoding is NOT encryption.

============================
JWT Decoding
============================

Anyone can decode:

• Header
• Payload

Without secret keys.

Only the signature protects integrity.

============================
Burp Suite JWT Testing
============================

Burp Suite provides JWT testing capabilities.

The JWT Editor extension helps:

• Decode JWTs
• Modify claims
• Change algorithms
• Sign tokens

Very useful during API testing.

============================
jwt.io
============================

jwt.io is a popular JWT analysis website.

It allows:

• JWT decoding
• Signature verification
• Token analysis

Important:

Never upload real production secrets or sensitive tokens.

============================
JWT Attacks in APIs
============================

JWT vulnerabilities commonly affect APIs.

Examples:

• Authorization bypass
• Admin access
• IDOR chaining
• Account takeover

Because APIs heavily rely on JWT authentication.

============================
Secure JWT Implementation
============================

============================
1. Strong Secret Keys
============================

Use strong random secrets.

Minimum:

256-bit random values.

============================
2. Restrict Algorithms
============================

Only allow expected algorithms.

Never trust:

alg from user-controlled headers.

============================
3. Disable alg:none
============================

Unsigned tokens should never be accepted.

============================
4. Validate All Claims
============================

Verify:

• exp
• iat
• issuer
• audience
• subject

============================
5. Short Expiration Times
============================

Reduce token lifetime.

============================
6. Secure Token Storage
============================

Prefer secure cookies with:

• HttpOnly
• Secure
• SameSite

============================
7. Rotate Secrets and Keys
============================

Regularly rotate:

• HMAC secrets
• RSA keys

============================
JWT Testing Methodology
============================

Security professionals commonly test:

• Header manipulation
• Algorithm confusion
• Weak secrets
• Claim tampering
• Expiration handling
• Signature validation
• Key injection attacks

============================
PortSwigger JWT Labs
============================

PortSwigger Web Security Academy provides practical JWT labs covering:

• alg:none bypass
• Weak signing keys
• JWK injection
• Algorithm confusion attacks

These labs simulate real-world JWT vulnerabilities.

============================
Why JWT Security Matters in Cybersecurity
============================

JWTs are deeply integrated into modern:

• APIs
• Mobile apps
• Cloud services
• Authentication systems

Weak JWT implementations may completely compromise authentication systems.

Understanding JWT attacks helps professionals:

• Secure APIs
• Detect token flaws
• Prevent authentication bypass
• Improve authorization security
• Build secure applications

JWT security is extremely important in:

• API Security
• Web Security
• Cloud Security
• DevSecOps
• Penetration Testing
• Bug Bounty Hunting

Modern applications heavily depend on tokens for authentication, making JWT security one of the most critical areas of modern cybersecurity.
keyPoints: JWTs are widely used for API authentication and authorization
JWTs contain header, payload, and signature sections
Base64 encoding is not encryption
alg:none vulnerabilities may allow unsigned token acceptance
Weak JWT secrets can be brute-forced
Algorithm confusion attacks exploit improper validation
JWT claims may expose sensitive information
JWK and kid injection attacks target key handling mechanisms
JWT Editor in Burp Suite helps analyze and manipulate tokens
Secure JWT implementations require strong secrets and strict validation
Short expiration times improve token security
JWT vulnerabilities may lead to authentication bypass and privilege escalation

content.theory:
File upload functionality is one of the most dangerous and commonly exploited features in web applications.

Modern applications allow users to upload:

• Profile pictures
• Documents
• PDFs
• Videos
• Attachments
• Resumes
• Images

Although file uploads improve functionality and user experience, insecure upload handling can lead to severe vulnerabilities.

Improper file upload security may allow attackers to:

• Upload malware
• Execute server-side code
• Bypass restrictions
• Gain remote access
• Perform path traversal
• Compromise servers

File upload vulnerabilities are extremely important in:

• Web Security
• Penetration Testing
• Bug Bounty Hunting
• Application Security
• Red Teaming

Understanding file upload attacks helps security professionals identify dangerous misconfigurations and improve secure application design.

============================
What is a File Upload Vulnerability?
============================

A file upload vulnerability occurs when applications improperly validate or securely handle uploaded files.

Applications may trust:

• File extensions
• MIME types
• Client-side validation

Attackers abuse weak validation to upload malicious files.

============================
Why File Upload Vulnerabilities are Dangerous
============================

Successful exploitation may lead to:

• Remote Code Execution (RCE)
• Webshell deployment
• Full server compromise
• Malware hosting
• Data theft
• Privilege escalation

File upload vulnerabilities are often considered critical.

============================
What is a Webshell?
============================

A webshell is a malicious script uploaded to a web server.

It allows attackers to execute commands remotely.

Webshells act as backdoors.

============================
Basic PHP Webshell Example
============================

<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>

If executed by the server:

Attackers may run operating system commands.

Example:

shell.php?cmd=whoami

The server executes:

whoami

============================
Why Webshells are Dangerous
============================

Attackers may:

• Read files
• Execute commands
• Download malware
• Create users
• Pivot inside networks
• Maintain persistence

Webshells often lead to complete compromise.

============================
How File Upload Features Work
============================

Typical upload workflow:

1. User selects a file.
2. Browser uploads file.
3. Server validates file.
4. File is stored.
5. File becomes accessible.

Weak validation creates security risks.

============================
Common File Upload Validation Methods
============================

Applications often validate:

• File extension
• MIME type
• File headers
• File size
• File content

Weak validation methods are easily bypassed.

============================
Client-Side Validation
============================

Client-side validation occurs in browsers using JavaScript.

Example:

Only allowing .jpg uploads.

Problem:

Attackers can bypass client-side validation easily.
