Assessment: Practical vulnerability testing lab (60 min) + vulnerability report submission
content.practicalTask: Final Month 2 Project — Choose one: OPTION A: Web Security Testing Toolkit (CLI Python tool combining 4+ tools). OPTION B: Automated Scanner Dashboard (Flask web UI with scan results). OPTION C: API Security Testing Framework (automated OWASP API Top 10 checker). All options require: Working code, README, 3-5 minute demo video, professional penetration test report.
content.assignment: Submit: (1) Practical lab screenshots. (2) Vulnerability report (professional format). (3) Final project code + demo video.

months.title: Advanced Security & Professional Penetration Testing
months.goal: Train students for advanced web security and structured penetration testing engagements.

content.theory:
Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) is one of the most dangerous modern web vulnerabilities.

Unlike many vulnerabilities that attack the client browser, SSRF targets the server itself.

In SSRF, an attacker tricks the server into making requests on the attacker's behalf.

This is extremely powerful because servers often have access to:

• Internal networks
• Cloud metadata services
• Private APIs
• Administrative panels
• Sensitive backend systems

Resources that are normally inaccessible from the internet may become reachable through SSRF.

SSRF vulnerabilities have caused:

• Massive cloud breaches
• Credential theft
• Internal network compromise
• Remote code execution

Modern applications frequently fetch external resources such as:

• Images
• Webhooks
• URLs
• PDFs
• API integrations

If user-controlled URLs are not validated properly, SSRF vulnerabilities may occur.

============================
What is SSRF?
============================

SSRF stands for:

Server-Side Request Forgery.

In SSRF:

The attacker controls a URL that the server fetches.

Instead of the server requesting a legitimate resource, the attacker forces it to request:

• Internal services
• Localhost
• Cloud metadata endpoints
• Private APIs

============================
How SSRF Works
============================

Normal behavior:

Application fetches external image:

https://example.com/image.jpg

Attacker changes URL to:

http://127.0.0.1

The server now makes a request to itself.

============================
Why SSRF is Dangerous
============================

Servers often trust internal systems.

Internal services may:

• Lack authentication
• Expose sensitive data
• Allow administrative actions

Attackers use SSRF to pivot into internal infrastructure.

============================
Common SSRF Targets
============================

Applications with URL-fetching functionality.

Examples:

• Image importers
• PDF generators
• URL previews
• Webhooks
• Video fetchers
• RSS readers
• Screenshot services

============================
Basic SSRF Testing
============================

If an application accepts URLs:

Try replacing the URL with:

http://127.0.0.1

or:

http://localhost

============================
Why localhost Matters
============================

localhost refers to:

The server itself.

SSRF may expose:

• Admin interfaces
• Internal APIs
• Debug panels

============================
Internal Network Access
============================

Many organizations use private IP ranges internally.

Examples:

• 10.x.x.x
• 172.16.x.x – 172.31.x.x
• 192.168.x.x

Normally inaccessible externally.

SSRF may allow attackers to scan these internal networks.

============================
Internal Service Discovery
============================

Attackers may use SSRF to identify:

• Databases
• Internal dashboards
• Redis servers
• Elasticsearch clusters
• Kubernetes APIs

============================
Cloud Metadata Services
============================

One of the most critical SSRF targets is:

Cloud metadata endpoints.

============================
AWS Metadata Endpoint
============================

Example:

http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/

This special IP address is used by:

Amazon EC2 instances.

============================
Why AWS Metadata Matters
============================

The metadata service may expose:

• IAM credentials
• Instance information
• Security tokens
• Access keys

If attackers obtain IAM credentials:

They may gain AWS API access.

============================
Potential AWS Impact
============================

Compromised IAM credentials may allow attackers to:

• Access S3 buckets
• Launch EC2 instances
• Read databases
• Modify infrastructure

============================
Cloud Providers with Metadata Services
============================

============================
Amazon Web Services (AWS)
============================

169.254.169.254

============================
Microsoft Azure
============================

Metadata service also exists.

============================
Google Cloud Platform (GCP)
============================

Provides metadata APIs as well.

============================
Blind SSRF
============================

Sometimes SSRF responses are not visible.

The server makes requests silently.

This is called:

Blind SSRF.

============================
Why Blind SSRF is Challenging
============================

Attackers cannot directly see responses.

Detection requires:

Out-of-band techniques.

============================
Out-of-Band (OOB) Detection
============================

Attackers provide URLs they control.

If the server requests the URL:

The attacker sees the callback.

============================
Burp Collaborator
============================

A tool provided by:

:contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}.

Used for:

• Blind SSRF detection
• Blind XSS detection
• OOB interactions

============================
How Burp Collaborator Works
============================

The tester generates a unique domain.

Example:

abcd1234.burpcollaborator.net

If the server requests it:

Burp logs the interaction.

============================
SSRF Bypass Techniques
============================

Applications often attempt to block SSRF.

Attackers use bypass methods.

============================
Alternative IP Representations
============================

127.0.0.1 may be represented differently.

Examples:

• 0x7f000001
• 2130706433
• 017700000001

Some filters fail to recognize these.

============================
DNS Rebinding
============================

DNS rebinding changes domain resolution dynamically.

Example:

Domain initially resolves externally.

Later resolves internally.

This may bypass hostname filtering.

============================
URL Parsing Inconsistencies
============================

Different parsers interpret URLs differently.

Examples:

• user@host confusion
• encoded characters
• mixed protocols

Attackers abuse parsing inconsistencies to bypass validation.

============================
SSRF to Remote Code Execution (RCE)
============================

SSRF sometimes reaches dangerous internal services.

Examples:

• Redis
• Elasticsearch
• Docker APIs
• Kubernetes APIs

Misconfigured internal services may allow:

• Command execution
• File writing
• Container compromise

============================
Redis Exploitation Example
============================

If Redis is exposed internally:

Attackers may write SSH keys or cron jobs.

Potentially leading to:

Remote code execution.

============================
SSRF in HTTP Headers
============================

Some applications process URLs inside headers.

Examples:

• Referer
• Host
• X-Forwarded-For

Improper validation may create SSRF opportunities.

============================
Host Header SSRF
============================

Applications sometimes build backend requests using the Host header.

Manipulating Host may redirect server-side traffic.

============================
URL Validation Mistakes
============================

Developers often validate:

• Strings only

Instead of:

• Actual resolved IP addresses.

This creates bypass opportunities.

============================
Defense Against SSRF
============================

============================
1. Domain Whitelisting
============================

Only allow trusted domains.

============================
2. Block Internal IP Ranges
============================

Reject:

• 127.0.0.1
• 10.x.x.x
• 172.16.x.x
• 192.168.x.x
• 169.254.169.254

============================
3. DNS Resolution Validation
============================

Validate the resolved IP before making requests.

============================
4. Disable Unnecessary URL Fetching
============================

Reduce attack surface.

============================
5. Network Segmentation
============================

Restrict internal service access.

============================
6. Cloud Metadata Protection
============================

AWS supports:

IMDSv2.

Adds stronger metadata protections.

============================
Why SSRF is Common in Modern Applications
============================

Modern applications frequently integrate with:

• External APIs
• Webhooks
• Cloud services
• Third-party resources

These integrations increase SSRF attack surface.

============================
Real-World SSRF Impact
============================

SSRF has been used in major breaches involving:

• Cloud credential theft
• Internal service compromise
• Infrastructure exposure

Because cloud infrastructure heavily relies on metadata services, SSRF is considered extremely high risk.

============================
Testing SSRF in Labs
============================

Safe practice environments include:

• DVWA
• PortSwigger Labs
• Hack The Box

Students should never test unauthorized systems.

============================
PortSwigger SSRF Labs
============================

The labs teach:

• Basic SSRF
• Backend system access
• Blind SSRF
• Burp Collaborator usage

============================
Importance of SSRF in Modern Cybersecurity
============================

SSRF is one of the most important vulnerabilities in:

• Cloud security
• Web application security
• API security

Understanding SSRF helps professionals:

• Identify dangerous URL-fetching logic
• Protect cloud infrastructure
• Secure internal services
• Detect metadata exposure risks

SSRF knowledge is extremely valuable for:

• Penetration Testing
• Bug Bounty Hunting
• Cloud Security
• Red Teaming
• Application Security

As cloud adoption continues increasing, SSRF remains one of the highest-impact vulnerabilities in modern cybersecurity.
keyPoints: SSRF tricks servers into making attacker-controlled requests
SSRF can access internal systems normally unreachable from the internet
Cloud metadata endpoints are high-value SSRF targets
AWS metadata exposure may leak IAM credentials
Blind SSRF requires out-of-band detection methods
Burp Collaborator helps detect blind SSRF
Alternative IP representations may bypass filters
SSRF may lead to remote code execution through internal services
Header-based SSRF vulnerabilities also exist
Proper validation and network restrictions help prevent SSRF
Modern cloud applications are especially vulnerable to SSRF
SSRF is one of the most impactful modern web vulnerabilities

content.theory:
XML External Entity (XXE) Injection is a powerful vulnerability that occurs when applications process XML input insecurely.

XXE vulnerabilities arise when XML parsers are configured to process external entities.

Attackers can abuse this behavior to:

• Read local files
• Perform SSRF attacks
• Access internal systems
• Exfiltrate sensitive data
• Cause denial of service
• Sometimes achieve remote code execution

XXE was historically extremely common in:

• SOAP services
• XML APIs
• File upload systems
• Enterprise applications

Although JSON is now more common than XML, many modern systems still process XML internally.

Understanding XXE remains extremely important in cybersecurity.

============================
What is XML?
============================

XML stands for:

Extensible Markup Language.

XML is used to structure and transport data.

It uses:

• Tags
• Attributes
• Nested elements

============================
Basic XML Example
============================

<user>
  <name>admin</name>
  <role>administrator</role>
</user>

============================
Why XML Exists
============================

XML was designed for:

• Data exchange
• Structured documents
• Machine-readable communication

============================
Where XML is Used
============================

XML is still used in:

• SOAP APIs
• Configuration files
• SAML authentication
• SVG images
• Office documents
• Android applications

============================
Understanding XML Parsers
============================

Applications use XML parsers to:

• Read XML
• Interpret XML structure
• Extract data

If parsers are configured insecurely:

XXE vulnerabilities may occur.

============================
What is an Entity?
============================

An entity is a variable-like placeholder in XML.

Entities can store:

• Text
• External resources
• File contents

============================
Example of an Entity
============================

<!ENTITY name "admin">

Used later as:

&name;

============================
DOCTYPE Declarations
============================

XML supports:

DOCTYPE declarations.

DOCTYPE allows defining entities.

============================
Basic DOCTYPE Example
============================

<!DOCTYPE foo [
  <!ENTITY test "hello">
]>

============================
What is an External Entity?
============================

An external entity references external resources.

Example:

<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">

The parser loads data from the file.

============================
Why External Entities are Dangerous
============================

Attackers may force the server to read:

• Sensitive files
• Internal URLs
• Cloud metadata

============================
Basic XXE Payload
============================

<!DOCTYPE foo [
<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">
]>
<foo>&xxe;</foo>

============================
What This Payload Does
============================

The parser reads:

/etc/passwd

Then inserts file contents into the XML response.

============================
Reading Local Files with XXE
============================

One of the most common XXE impacts is:

Local file disclosure.

============================
Common Linux Targets
============================

• /etc/passwd
• /etc/shadow
• Application config files
• SSH keys
• Environment variables

============================
Why /etc/passwd Matters
============================

It contains:

• System users
• Service accounts
• Environment information

Useful for reconnaissance.

============================
XXE in Web Applications
============================

Applications may accept XML through:

• POST requests
• File uploads
• SOAP APIs
• API integrations

============================
SOAP and XXE
============================

SOAP heavily relies on XML.

Historically, many SOAP services were vulnerable to XXE.

============================
XXE to SSRF
============================

XXE can also perform:

Server-Side Request Forgery.

Instead of file://

Attackers use:

http://

============================
SSRF XXE Example
============================

<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "http://127.0.0.1">

The server makes an HTTP request internally.

============================
Why XXE-to-SSRF is Dangerous
============================

Attackers may access:

• Internal APIs
• Cloud metadata
• Admin panels
• Databases

============================
Blind XXE
============================

Sometimes the application does not return XML output.

This is called:

Blind XXE.

============================
Why Blind XXE is Challenging
============================

Attackers cannot directly see file contents.

Alternative techniques are required.

============================
Out-of-Band (OOB) XXE
============================

Blind XXE often uses:

Out-of-band exfiltration.

The server contacts an attacker-controlled system.

============================
DNS Exfiltration
============================

The XML parser triggers DNS lookups.

The attacker observes the requests externally.

============================
HTTP Exfiltration
============================

The parser sends HTTP requests to attacker infrastructure.

Useful for:

• Blind XXE confirmation
• Data extraction

============================
Burp Collaborator and XXE
============================

:contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0} Burp Collaborator is commonly used for:

• Blind XXE
• Blind SSRF
• OOB detection

============================
Blind XXE via Error Messages
============================

Some XML parsers include file contents inside parser errors.

Attackers intentionally trigger parsing failures.

============================
Why Error Messages Matter
============================

Verbose parser errors may leak:

• File contents
• Internal paths
• Server configuration

============================
XXE in File Uploads
============================

Some uploaded file formats internally use XML.

Examples:

• SVG images
• DOCX
• XLSX
• Android APKs

============================
SVG XXE Attacks
============================

SVG images are XML-based.

Malicious SVG uploads may trigger XXE.

============================
Office Document XXE
============================

Modern Office formats contain XML internally.

Improper parsing may expose XXE vulnerabilities.

============================
XXE in SOAP Services
============================

SOAP uses XML envelopes.

If entity processing is enabled:

SOAP endpoints may become vulnerable.

============================
Content-Type Manipulation
============================

Some applications expect JSON.

Attackers may change:

Content-Type: application/xml

Then send XML payloads.

============================
Why Content-Type Testing Matters
============================

Hidden XML parsers may exist behind APIs.

Unexpected parser behavior may expose XXE.

============================
XInclude Attacks
============================

Sometimes attackers cannot modify the DOCTYPE.

But they may still control partial XML.

============================
What is XInclude?
============================

XInclude allows XML documents to include external resources.

============================
Basic XInclude Payload
============================

<xi:include href="file:///etc/passwd" parse="text" xmlns:xi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XInclude"/>

============================
Why XInclude is Dangerous
============================

Even without DOCTYPE control:

Attackers may still read files.

============================
Denial of Service via XML
============================

XML parsers may also be vulnerable to:

Resource exhaustion attacks.

============================
Billion Laughs Attack
============================

An XML payload recursively expands entities.

This consumes:

• CPU
• Memory

Potentially crashing servers.

============================
Why XML Parsers are High Risk
============================

XML parsers often:

• Access local files
• Resolve URLs
• Support entity expansion

These features increase attack surface.

============================
Defending Against XXE
============================

============================
1. Disable External Entity Processing
============================

Most important defense.

Disable:

• External entities
• DTD processing

============================
2. Use Secure Parser Configurations
============================

Modern parsers support secure modes.

============================
3. Prefer JSON Where Possible
============================

JSON avoids many XML-specific risks.

============================
4. Validate XML Input
============================

Reject unexpected XML structures.

============================
5. Limit Network Access
============================

Prevent parsers from reaching internal systems.

============================
6. Restrict File Access
============================

Use least privilege permissions.

============================
Why XXE Still Matters
============================

Even though JSON is popular:

Many enterprise systems still rely on XML.

Examples:

• Banking systems
• Government applications
• SOAP services
• SAML authentication

============================
Real-World XXE Impact
============================

XXE vulnerabilities have exposed:

• Cloud credentials
• Sensitive configuration files
• Internal services
• Database credentials

Some breaches began with simple XML parser misconfigurations.

============================
PortSwigger XXE Labs
============================

These labs teach:

• File disclosure
• SSRF through XXE
• Blind XXE
• OOB exploitation

============================
Importance of XXE in Cybersecurity
============================

Understanding XXE helps professionals:

• Secure XML parsers
• Detect dangerous parser behavior
• Protect cloud infrastructure
• Prevent SSRF and file disclosure

XXE knowledge is valuable in:

• Web Application Security
• API Security
• Penetration Testing
• Cloud Security
• Red Teaming

Even though XML is older technology, insecure XML processing remains a critical security issue in many modern enterprise environments.

Understanding XXE therefore remains an essential skill for cybersecurity professionals.
keyPoints: XXE occurs when XML parsers process external entities insecurely
Attackers can read local files using XXE payloads
XXE may also perform SSRF attacks against internal systems
Blind XXE uses out-of-band techniques for detection and exfiltration
SOAP services and XML APIs are common XXE targets
SVG and Office file uploads may also contain XML
XInclude attacks work even when DOCTYPE control is unavailable
Verbose parser errors may leak sensitive information
Disabling external entity processing is the primary defense
JSON reduces many XML-specific security risks
Cloud infrastructure may be exposed through XXE-to-SSRF attacks
XXE remains important in modern enterprise security

content.theory:
Insecure deserialization is one of the most dangerous vulnerability classes in modern applications.

It occurs when applications deserialize untrusted or attacker-controlled data without proper validation.

This vulnerability can lead to:

• Authentication bypass
• Privilege escalation
• File manipulation
• Denial of service
• Remote code execution (RCE)

Deserialization vulnerabilities are especially dangerous because exploitation often results in complete server compromise.

OWASP has consistently ranked insecure deserialization among the most critical web security risks.

Understanding deserialization requires first understanding how applications store and transfer objects.

============================
What is Serialization?
============================

Serialization is the process of converting an object into a format that can be:

• Stored
• Transmitted
• Saved to disk
• Sent across networks

Applications serialize objects for:

• Sessions
• Caching
• APIs
• File storage
• Inter-process communication

============================
Example of an Object
============================

An object may contain:

• Username
• Role
• Permissions
• Session data

Example:

User {
  username: "admin"
  role: "administrator"
}

============================
Serialized Object
============================

The object is transformed into:

• Bytes
• JSON
• XML
• Binary data

So it can be transmitted or stored.

============================
What is Deserialization?
============================

Deserialization is the reverse process.

The application reconstructs the object from serialized data.

Example:

Serialized data → User object recreated in memory.

============================
Why Serialization Exists
============================

Serialization helps applications:

• Maintain sessions
• Share objects between systems
• Cache data efficiently
• Save application state

============================
Why Deserialization Becomes Dangerous
============================

The danger appears when:

Attackers can modify serialized data before deserialization.

If applications trust attacker-controlled serialized objects:

Attackers may manipulate:

• Object properties
• Object types
• Application logic

============================
What Makes Deserialization So Dangerous?
============================

Unlike simple input validation bugs:

Deserialization may directly affect:

• Program execution flow
• Internal classes
• Memory objects

In many cases, this leads directly to:

Remote code execution.

============================
Understanding Object Injection
============================

Object injection occurs when attackers inject malicious serialized objects.

These objects may trigger dangerous behavior during deserialization.

============================
Common Deserialization Targets
============================

Serialized data is often found in:

• Cookies
• Session tokens
• Hidden form fields
• POST bodies
• API requests
• Cache systems

============================
How Attackers Identify Serialized Data
============================

Attackers look for:

• Base64 encoded blobs
• Strange binary data
• Structured object formats

============================
Java Serialized Objects
============================

Java serialization is common in:

• Java EE applications
• Enterprise middleware
• Legacy systems

============================
Java Serialization Magic Bytes
============================

Serialized Java objects often begin with:

rO0AB

When Base64 encoded.

============================
Why This Matters
============================

This pattern helps testers recognize:

Potential Java deserialization.

============================
PHP Deserialization
============================

PHP applications commonly use:

serialize()

and:

unserialize()

============================
Why PHP Deserialization is Dangerous
============================

If attacker-controlled data reaches:

unserialize()

Attackers may exploit:

Magic methods.

============================
PHP Magic Methods
============================

Special PHP methods execute automatically.

Examples:

• __wakeup()
• __destruct()
• __toString()

============================
Why Magic Methods Matter
============================

Attackers abuse these methods to:

• Execute commands
• Delete files
• Write files
• Trigger dangerous functions

============================
POP Chains
============================

POP stands for:

Property-Oriented Programming.

Attackers chain together existing classes and methods to achieve code execution.

============================
How POP Chains Work
============================

The attacker:

• Manipulates object properties
• Triggers magic methods
• Reuses existing application code

This leads to dangerous behavior.

============================
Python Deserialization
============================

Python applications often use:

pickle

for serialization.

============================
Why pickle is Dangerous
============================

pickle.loads(user_data)

Can execute arbitrary code during deserialization.

============================
Why Python pickle is High Risk
============================

pickle is not designed for untrusted data.

It allows:

• Arbitrary object reconstruction
• Code execution during loading

============================
Java Gadget Chains
============================

Java deserialization attacks often use:

Gadget chains.

============================
What is a Gadget?
============================

A gadget is:

A piece of existing code that performs useful actions during deserialization.

============================
Gadget Chains
============================

Multiple gadgets are chained together to:

• Execute commands
• Write files
• Download payloads

============================
Commons Collections Exploits
============================

One famous Java deserialization attack uses:

Apache Commons Collections.

============================
ysoserial
============================

A popular Java deserialization exploitation framework.

Created by:

Security researchers for testing and education.

============================
Basic ysoserial Example
============================

java -jar ysoserial.jar CommonsCollections1 "cmd"

============================
What ysoserial Does
============================

Generates malicious serialized Java payloads.

Useful for:

• Security testing
• Research
• Education

============================
PHPGGC
============================

PHPGGC is similar to ysoserial.

But for:

PHP gadget chains.

============================
Why Gadget Chain Tools Matter
============================

Applications often contain:

• Dangerous classes
• Existing gadgets
• Exploitable libraries

Attackers automate payload generation.

============================
Type Manipulation Attacks
============================

Attackers may change:

• Integer → Boolean
• User → Admin
• False → True

During deserialization.

============================
PHP Type Juggling
============================

PHP loosely compares values.

This may create:

Authentication bypasses.

============================
Example of Dangerous Logic
============================

if ($user == true)

Improper comparisons may be bypassed.

============================
Deserialization and Sessions
============================

Some applications store session objects directly.

Attackers may modify serialized session data to:

• Escalate privileges
• Change usernames
• Become administrators

============================
Modifying Serialized Cookies
============================

A common attack involves:

• Decoding serialized cookie
• Editing object values
• Re-encoding and sending back

============================
How Attackers Detect Serialization
============================

Common indicators:

• Base64 blobs
• Serialized syntax
• Predictable structures

============================
Common Serialization Formats
============================

============================
Java
============================

Binary serialized objects.

============================
PHP
============================

O:4:"User":2:{...}

============================
Python pickle
============================

Binary pickle data.

============================
JSON Serialization
============================

Generally safer.

But dangerous if used insecurely.

============================
Remote Code Execution (RCE)
============================

The worst-case deserialization impact is:

Remote Code Execution.

Attackers may gain:

• Full server control
• Shell access
• Persistence

============================
Why RCE is Critical
============================

RCE often leads to:

• Data breaches
• Lateral movement
• Infrastructure compromise

============================
Deserialization in Enterprise Systems
============================

Large enterprise systems frequently use:

• Java serialization
• SOAP
• Legacy middleware

Making deserialization vulnerabilities especially dangerous in enterprise environments.

============================
Famous Deserialization Vulnerabilities
============================

Several major enterprise products were compromised through:

• Java gadget chains
• Unsafe deserialization
• Middleware vulnerabilities

============================
Defense Against Insecure Deserialization
============================

============================
1. Never Deserialize Untrusted Data
============================

Most important defense.

============================
2. Use Safe Data Formats
============================

Prefer:

• JSON
• Primitive data structures

Instead of raw object serialization.

============================
3. Implement Class Allowlists
============================

Restrict which classes may be deserialized.

============================
4. Digitally Sign Serialized Data
============================

Prevents attacker modification.

============================
5. Encrypt Serialized Data
============================

Protects confidentiality and integrity.

============================
6. Avoid Dangerous Libraries
============================

Remove unused gadget chains.

============================
7. Update Dependencies
============================

Many deserialization vulnerabilities rely on outdated libraries.

============================
Why Modern Applications Still Use Serialization
============================

Serialization remains useful for:

• Performance
• Distributed systems
• Session handling
• Messaging systems

But it must be implemented securely.

============================
PortSwigger Deserialization Labs
============================

These labs teach:

• Serialized object modification
• Type manipulation
• PHP object injection
• Privilege escalation

============================
Why Deserialization Matters in Cybersecurity
============================

Deserialization vulnerabilities are extremely high impact.

Understanding insecure deserialization helps professionals:

• Detect dangerous object handling
• Identify unsafe libraries
• Prevent RCE vulnerabilities
• Secure session mechanisms

Deserialization knowledge is highly valuable in:

• Web Application Security
• Enterprise Security
• Penetration Testing
• Red Teaming
• Security Research

Because insecure deserialization can directly lead to complete application compromise, it remains one of the most critical vulnerability classes in modern cybersecurity.
keyPoints: Serialization converts objects into storable or transmittable formats
Deserialization reconstructs objects from serialized data
Insecure deserialization occurs when untrusted data is deserialized
Deserialization vulnerabilities often lead to remote code execution
Java serialized objects commonly begin with rO0AB
PHP unserialize() is dangerous with attacker-controlled input
Python pickle is unsafe for untrusted data
Magic methods in PHP can trigger dangerous behavior automatically
Gadget chains are used to achieve code execution during deserialization
ysoserial and PHPGGC generate deserialization payloads
Serialized objects are commonly found in cookies and session tokens
Class allowlists and signed data help defend against deserialization attacks

content.theory:
Modern web applications rely heavily on JavaScript frameworks, caching systems, and performance optimization technologies.

While these technologies improve speed and functionality, they also introduce entirely new categories of vulnerabilities.

Two important modern web vulnerabilities are:

• Prototype Pollution
• Web Cache Poisoning

Prototype Pollution targets how JavaScript objects inherit properties.

Web Cache Poisoning targets caching mechanisms used by websites and CDNs.

Both vulnerabilities are especially dangerous because:

• They can affect many users simultaneously
• They are difficult to detect
• They often impact modern frameworks and APIs
• They may lead to account compromise, XSS, or even remote code execution

Understanding these vulnerabilities is important for modern web application security testing.

============================
PART 1 — PROTOTYPE POLLUTION
============================

============================
What is JavaScript Prototype Inheritance?
============================

JavaScript uses:

Prototype-based inheritance.

Every JavaScript object inherits properties from:

Object.prototype

============================
Basic Example
============================

If we create:

const user = {}

The object automatically inherits methods like:

• toString()
• hasOwnProperty()

From:

Object.prototype

============================
Why Prototypes Matter
============================

Because all objects inherit from Object.prototype:

Changing the prototype affects ALL objects.

============================
What is Prototype Pollution?
============================

Prototype Pollution occurs when attackers inject properties into:

Object.prototype

This globally modifies application behavior.

============================
Basic Prototype Pollution Concept
============================

If an attacker injects:

__proto__.isAdmin = true

Then:

Every object may inherit:

isAdmin = true

============================
Why This is Dangerous
============================

Applications may trust inherited properties.

This can lead to:

• Authentication bypass
• Privilege escalation
• XSS
• Remote code execution

============================
Understanding __proto__
============================

__proto__ is a JavaScript property pointing to an object's prototype.

Example:

obj.__proto__

============================
Dangerous Input Handling
============================

Applications sometimes merge user-controlled JSON into objects.

Example:

Object.assign(target, userInput)

If userInput contains:

{
  "__proto__": {
    "isAdmin": true
  }
}

Prototype pollution may occur.

============================
How Pollution Spreads
============================

Once polluted:

All newly created objects may inherit malicious properties.

============================
Example Attack Scenario
============================

Application checks:

if (user.isAdmin)

Attacker pollutes prototype:

Object.prototype.isAdmin = true

Now all users may appear as administrators.

============================
Client-Side Prototype Pollution
============================

Occurs inside the browser.

Usually affects:

• Frontend JavaScript
• Browser behavior
• DOM manipulation

============================
Impact of Client-Side Pollution
============================

May lead to:

• Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
• UI manipulation
• Security bypasses

============================
DOM-Based XSS and Pollution
============================

Polluted properties may eventually reach:

• innerHTML
• eval()
• dangerous DOM sinks

Leading to JavaScript execution.

============================
Server-Side Prototype Pollution
============================

Occurs in:

Node.js applications.

Much more dangerous.

============================
Why Server-Side Pollution is Critical
============================

Server-side pollution may affect:

• Authentication logic
• File operations
• Process execution
• Security middleware

============================
Prototype Pollution to RCE
============================

If polluted properties reach dangerous functions like:

child_process.exec()

Attackers may achieve:

Remote code execution.

============================
Common Pollution Sources
============================

Applications processing:

• JSON input
• Query parameters
• Deep merge functions
• YAML
• API payloads

============================
Dangerous JavaScript Libraries
============================

Some older libraries were vulnerable due to insecure object merging.

Examples historically included:

• lodash
• jQuery plugins
• merge utilities

============================
Common Dangerous Keys
============================

Attackers commonly test:

• __proto__
• constructor
• prototype

============================
Prototype Pollution Detection
============================

A common test:

Send payload containing:

{
  "__proto__": {
    "polluted": "yes"
  }
}

Then check:

({}).polluted

============================
Browser Console Testing
============================

In DevTools:

Object.prototype.polluted = 'yes'

Then:

({}).polluted

Returns:

"yes"

============================
Why This Demonstrates Pollution
============================

Because every object now inherits:

polluted = "yes"

============================
Prototype Pollution Challenges
============================

Detection can be difficult because:

• Impact varies
• Some applications sanitize input partially
• Pollution effects may appear later

============================
Defense Against Prototype Pollution
============================

============================
1. Sanitize Dangerous Keys
============================

Reject:

• __proto__
• constructor
• prototype

============================
2. Avoid Unsafe Merge Functions
============================

Use secure object merging.

============================
3. Freeze Prototypes
============================

Prevent runtime modification.

============================
4. Update Dependencies
============================

Many vulnerabilities exist in outdated libraries.

============================
5. Validate JSON Structures
============================

Strict schema validation reduces risk.

============================
PART 2 — WEB CACHE POISONING
============================

Modern websites use caching systems to improve speed and reduce server load.

Caching systems store responses temporarily.

Future users receive cached responses instead of generating new ones.

============================
What is a Cache?
============================

A cache stores previously generated responses.

Examples:

• HTML pages
• Images
• API responses

============================
Why Caches Exist
============================

Caching improves:

• Speed
• Scalability
• Performance

============================
Common Cache Systems
============================

Examples include:

• CDNs
• Reverse proxies
• Web accelerators

============================
Popular Caching Technologies
============================

• Varnish
• Cloudflare
• Akamai
• Nginx caching

============================
What is Web Cache Poisoning?
============================

Web Cache Poisoning occurs when attackers trick the cache into storing malicious responses.

Other users then receive the poisoned response.

============================
Why Cache Poisoning is Dangerous
============================

A single poisoned response may impact:

• Thousands of users
• Entire websites
• Cached API responses

============================
How Caches Identify Responses
============================

Caches use:

Cache Keys.

The cache key determines whether a cached response should be reused.

============================
What is a Cache Key?
============================

The cache key identifies unique requests.

Typically includes:

• URL
• Path
• Sometimes headers

============================
Unkeyed Inputs
============================

Some request components affect responses but are NOT included in the cache key.

These are called:

Unkeyed inputs.

============================
Why Unkeyed Inputs are Dangerous
============================

Attackers manipulate unkeyed values.

The poisoned response gets cached.

Future users receive the malicious response.

============================
Common Unkeyed Headers
============================

Examples:

• X-Forwarded-Host
• X-Original-URL
• X-Host
• X-Rewrite-URL

============================
Example Cache Poisoning Attack
============================

Attacker sends:

X-Forwarded-Host: evil.com

If the application reflects the header:

The malicious content may become cached.

============================
Impact of Cache Poisoning
============================

May lead to:

• Stored XSS
• Redirect attacks
• Session theft
• Malware delivery

============================
Cache Poisoning and XSS
============================

One dangerous scenario:

Attacker injects JavaScript into cached responses.

All future users execute the payload.

============================
Unkeyed Query Parameters
============================

Some applications reflect query parameters without including them in cache keys.

Attackers abuse this behavior.

============================
Cache Poisoning Workflow
============================

1. Identify cache behavior
2. Find unkeyed input
3. Inject malicious payload
4. Trigger caching
5. Victims receive poisoned response

============================
Detecting Cache Poisoning
============================

Indicators include:

• X-Cache headers
• Age headers
• CDN-specific headers

============================
Cache Header Examples
============================

• X-Cache: HIT
• CF-Cache-Status: HIT

These indicate caching activity.

============================
PortSwigger Cache Poisoning Labs
============================

The labs teach:

• Unkeyed headers
• Unkeyed query strings
• Cache behavior analysis

============================
Defense Against Cache Poisoning
============================

============================
1. Include Relevant Inputs in Cache Keys
============================

Prevent cache confusion.

============================
2. Avoid Reflecting Untrusted Headers
============================

Reduce attack surface.

============================
3. Validate User Input
============================

Reject malicious payloads.

============================
4. Configure Cache Rules Carefully
============================

Ensure safe caching logic.

============================
5. Disable Caching for Sensitive Responses
============================

Protect dynamic content.

============================
Prototype Pollution vs Cache Poisoning
============================

============================
Prototype Pollution
============================

Targets:

JavaScript object inheritance.

============================
Web Cache Poisoning
============================

Targets:

Caching infrastructure.

============================
Why These Vulnerabilities Matter
============================

Both vulnerabilities affect:

• Modern frameworks
• Large-scale applications
• Cloud environments

And both may impact many users simultaneously.

============================
Importance in Modern Cybersecurity
============================

Modern web applications increasingly depend on:

• JavaScript frameworks
• APIs
• CDNs
• Caching systems

Understanding Prototype Pollution and Web Cache Poisoning helps professionals:

• Secure modern JavaScript applications
• Detect hidden cache vulnerabilities
• Prevent large-scale attacks
• Understand advanced web exploitation techniques

These vulnerabilities are highly valuable knowledge areas in:

• Bug Bounty Hunting
• Web Application Security
• Red Teaming
• Application Security Engineering
• Security Research

As web technologies continue evolving, these advanced vulnerability classes are becoming increasingly important in modern cybersecurity testing.
keyPoints: Prototype Pollution modifies JavaScript object inheritance globally
Polluting Object.prototype affects all JavaScript objects
Client-side prototype pollution may lead to XSS
Server-side prototype pollution in Node.js may lead to RCE
Dangerous keys include __proto__, constructor, and prototype
Web caches store responses to improve performance
Web Cache Poisoning tricks caches into storing malicious responses
Unkeyed headers and query strings are common cache poisoning vectors
Cache poisoning may impact many users simultaneously
X-Forwarded-Host is a common unkeyed header target
Secure input validation and proper cache configuration reduce risk
These vulnerabilities are highly relevant in modern web applications
resources: PortSwigger Web Cache Poisoning Learning Path

content.theory:
Race conditions are advanced vulnerabilities that occur when applications process multiple requests simultaneously in an unsafe way.

These vulnerabilities exploit timing windows inside application logic.

Instead of attacking weak input validation or broken authentication directly, race condition attacks abuse:

• Concurrency
• Timing
• Parallel request handling

Race condition vulnerabilities can allow attackers to:

• Bypass rate limits
• Double-spend funds
• Redeem coupons multiple times
• Create duplicate accounts
• Abuse one-time tokens
• Escalate privileges

Race conditions are among the most difficult vulnerabilities to:

• Detect
• Reproduce
• Fix

Because the vulnerability depends heavily on timing and server behavior.

Understanding race conditions is important for advanced web security testing and modern application security.

============================
What is a Race Condition?
============================

A race condition occurs when:

Multiple operations execute concurrently and the final result depends on execution timing.

The application assumes requests will execute sequentially.

Attackers exploit situations where requests execute simultaneously.

============================
Simple Real-World Analogy
============================

Imagine:

Two people attempt to buy the last concert ticket at the exact same moment.

If the system checks ticket availability before updating inventory:

Both purchases may succeed.

This is a race condition.

============================
Why Race Conditions Happen
============================

Modern servers process:

• Multiple threads
• Multiple requests
• Parallel operations

Without proper synchronization:

Unexpected behavior occurs.

============================
Concurrent Requests
============================

Race conditions rely on:

Concurrent requests.

Attackers intentionally send many requests at nearly the same time.

============================
What Makes Race Conditions Dangerous
============================

Applications often assume operations happen:

One at a time.

Attackers break this assumption.

============================
Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU)
============================

One of the most common race condition concepts.

============================
Understanding TOCTOU
============================

The application:

1. Checks a condition
2. Uses the result later

Between these two steps:

The application state may change.

============================
Example TOCTOU Scenario
============================

Application checks:

Is coupon valid?

Before marking it as used:

Another request redeems the same coupon.

Both succeed.

============================
Common Race Condition Targets
============================

Race conditions frequently appear in:

• Payment systems
• Coupon redemption
• Wallet transfers
• Inventory systems
• Voting systems
• One-time password systems
• Password reset flows

============================
Double-Spending Example
============================

Attacker sends two transfer requests simultaneously.

Both requests check:

Balance = sufficient.

Before balance updates.

Result:

Money spent twice.

============================
Coupon Abuse Example
============================

Coupon should work:

Once.

Attacker sends:

50 simultaneous redemption requests.

Several succeed before coupon invalidation.

============================
Rate Limit Bypass
============================

Applications may limit:

• Login attempts
• OTP submissions
• API requests

Race conditions may bypass these protections.

============================
Why Rate Limits Fail
============================

The server checks:

Request count.

But updates the counter too late.

Simultaneous requests bypass restrictions.

============================
Race Conditions in Authentication
============================

Examples include:

• OTP reuse
• MFA bypass
• Password reset token reuse
• Multiple session creation

============================
Burp Suite and Race Conditions
============================

:contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0} Burp Suite supports race condition testing.

============================
Burp Repeater Parallel Requests
============================

Burp Repeater includes:

Send group in parallel.

This sends requests simultaneously.

Useful for:

• Coupon testing
• OTP testing
• Account creation races

============================
Why Parallel Sending Matters
============================

Precise timing is critical.

Sequential requests may not trigger vulnerabilities.

============================
Turbo Intruder
============================

A powerful Burp extension designed for:

High-speed request attacks.

============================
Turbo Intruder Features
============================

• Massive concurrency
• Precise timing
• Low-level request control
• HTTP/2 support

============================
Why Turbo Intruder is Important
============================

Some race conditions require:

Extremely accurate synchronization.

Turbo Intruder enables advanced testing.

============================
HTTP/2 and Race Conditions
============================

HTTP/2 introduced:

Multiplexing.

Multiple requests travel over one connection simultaneously.

============================
HTTP/2 Single-Packet Attack
============================

An advanced technique where:

Multiple requests are sent inside a single TCP packet.

============================
Why Single-Packet Attacks Matter
============================

This minimizes:

• Network latency
• Timing inconsistencies
• Packet jitter

Allowing highly reliable race condition exploitation.

============================
What is Network Jitter?
============================

Small timing variations during network transmission.

These variations can prevent race conditions from triggering.

============================
Finding Race Conditions
============================

Look for operations intended to happen:

Only once.

============================
High-Risk Functionalities
============================

• Coupon redemption
• Gift cards
• Referral rewards
• Money transfers
• OTP verification
• Vote systems
• Inventory purchases

============================
Race Condition Testing Methodology
============================

============================
1. Identify Sensitive Operation
============================

Find actions that modify state.

============================
2. Capture Request
============================

Use Burp Proxy.

============================
3. Duplicate Requests
============================

Prepare multiple identical requests.

============================
4. Send Simultaneously
============================

Use parallel sending.

============================
5. Analyze Results
============================

Check whether:

• Multiple actions succeeded
• Counters broke
• Limits bypassed

============================
Indicators of Vulnerability
============================

• Duplicate redemptions
• Multiple successful responses
• Negative balances
• Reused tokens

============================
Atomic Operations
============================

Secure systems use:

Atomic operations.

============================
What is an Atomic Operation?
============================

An operation that completes entirely:

Without interruption.

============================
Why Atomicity Prevents Race Conditions
============================

No other request can modify state during execution.

============================
Database Locks
============================

Applications often use:

Database-level locking.

To prevent concurrent modifications.

============================
Types of Locks
============================

• Row locks
• Table locks
• Transactions

============================
Transactions
============================

Databases group operations into:

Transactions.

If one step fails:

All changes rollback.

============================
Race Conditions in APIs
============================

Modern APIs are especially vulnerable because:

• APIs process requests rapidly
• Parallel calls are common
• Microservices increase complexity

============================
Distributed Systems and Races
============================

Cloud-native systems introduce:

• Multiple services
• Queues
• Async processing

Increasing race condition risks.

============================
Complex Attack Chains
============================

Advanced attackers combine vulnerabilities.

Example:

• SSRF → Internal access
• Race condition → Privilege escalation
• Deserialization → RCE

============================
Why Attack Chaining Matters
============================

Real-world attacks rarely use:

One vulnerability alone.

Attackers chain weaknesses together.

============================
Complex Attack Simulation
============================

Security professionals simulate:

Full attack scenarios.

To understand:

• Business impact
• Attack paths
• Lateral movement

============================
Professional Reporting
============================

Complex attack reports should include:

• Vulnerability chain
• Exploitation steps
• CVSS scores
• Business impact
• Screenshots
• Remediation guidance

============================
CVSS and Race Conditions
============================

Race condition severity depends on:

• Financial impact
• Authentication bypass
• Data exposure
• Exploit reliability

============================
Business Impact Examples
============================

• Unlimited coupon redemption
• Financial fraud
• Resource exhaustion
• Account compromise

============================
PortSwigger Race Condition Labs
============================

These labs teach:

• Parallel request exploitation
• Multi-endpoint races
• Rate limit bypass
• Timing attacks

============================
James Kettle Research
============================

:contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1} published major research on:

• HTTP/2 race conditions
• Single-packet attacks
• Web timing exploitation

His work significantly advanced modern race condition testing.

============================
Defense Against Race Conditions
============================

============================
1. Atomic Operations
============================

Critical state changes should be atomic.

============================
2. Database Transactions
============================

Use transactional integrity.

============================
3. Proper Locking
============================

Prevent simultaneous modification.

============================
4. Idempotency Tokens
============================

Ensure operations execute once.

============================
5. Queue Processing
============================

Serialize sensitive operations.

============================
6. Rate Limiting at Infrastructure Level
============================

Protect APIs and authentication flows.

============================
Why Race Conditions Matter in Modern Security
============================

Modern applications are:

• Highly concurrent
• Distributed
• API-driven
• Cloud-native

This increases race condition exposure.

============================
Importance in Cybersecurity
============================

Understanding race conditions helps professionals:

• Secure payment systems
• Protect authentication flows
• Prevent financial abuse
• Test concurrent systems safely

Race condition knowledge is highly valuable in:

• Bug Bounty Hunting
• Application Security
• Red Teaming
• API Security
• Financial Technology Security

Because race conditions exploit application logic itself rather than simple input validation flaws, they represent one of the most advanced and impactful categories of modern web vulnerabilities.
keyPoints: Race conditions exploit timing windows in concurrent operations
TOCTOU vulnerabilities occur between checking and using data
Simultaneous requests may bypass application logic protections
Race conditions commonly affect payment and coupon systems
Burp Suite supports parallel request testing
Turbo Intruder enables high-precision race condition attacks
HTTP/2 single-packet attacks improve synchronization accuracy
Atomic operations and database locks prevent race conditions
Modern APIs and distributed systems increase race condition risks
Complex attack chains combine multiple vulnerabilities together
Proper reporting should include CVSS and business impact
Race conditions are among the most advanced modern web vulnerabilities

content.theory:
OAuth 2.0 is the industry-standard authorization framework used by modern applications to allow users to log in using third-party providers such as:

• Google
• GitHub
• Facebook
• Microsoft
• LinkedIn

When users click:

“Login with Google”

They are usually using OAuth 2.0.

OAuth is one of the most widely used authentication and authorization technologies on the internet.

However, OAuth is also extremely complex.

Incorrect implementations frequently introduce vulnerabilities that can lead to:

• Account takeover
• Authentication bypass
• Token theft
• Session hijacking

Understanding OAuth security is critical for modern web and API security testing.

============================
What is OAuth 2.0?
============================

OAuth 2.0 is an:

Authorization framework.

It allows applications to access resources on behalf of users without sharing passwords directly.

============================
Why OAuth Exists
============================

Without OAuth:

Users would need to give their passwords directly to third-party applications.

This is dangerous.

OAuth solves this by:

Using tokens instead of passwords.

============================
Example OAuth Scenario
============================

A website wants access to your:

Google profile.

Instead of sharing your Google password:

Google provides an authorization token.

============================
Authorization vs Authentication
============================

OAuth was originally designed for:

Authorization.

Not authentication.

============================
What is Authorization?
============================

Authorization determines:

What a user or application is allowed to access.

============================
What is Authentication?
============================

Authentication verifies:

Who the user is.

============================
OAuth and Login Systems
============================

Modern applications commonly use OAuth for:

Authentication.

This creates additional security risks.

============================
OAuth 2.0 Roles
============================

OAuth defines several important roles.

============================
1. Resource Owner
============================

Usually:

The user.

============================
2. Client
============================

The application requesting access.

Example:

A website using “Login with Google”.

============================
3. Authorization Server
============================

The system authenticating users.

Examples:

• Google
• GitHub
• Facebook

============================
4. Resource Server
============================

The API holding protected data.

============================
OAuth Tokens
============================

OAuth uses:

Access tokens.

Instead of passwords.

============================
What Access Tokens Do
============================

Tokens allow applications to:

• Access APIs
• Read profile data
• Perform authorized actions

============================
Scopes in OAuth
============================

Scopes define:

What access is granted.

Examples:

• email
• profile
• contacts

============================
Why Scopes Matter
============================

Applications should request:

Only necessary permissions.

============================
OAuth Authorization Code Flow
============================

The most secure and commonly used OAuth flow.

============================
Authorization Code Flow Steps
============================

1. User clicks Login with Google
2. Browser redirects to Google
3. User authenticates
4. Google sends authorization code
5. Application exchanges code for token
6. Application accesses API

============================
Why Authorization Codes Exist
============================

The authorization code acts as:

A temporary credential.

Instead of exposing tokens directly.

============================
OAuth Redirects
============================

OAuth relies heavily on:

Browser redirects.

This creates many security risks.

============================
redirect_uri Parameter
============================

Defines:

Where the authorization server sends users after authentication.

============================
Why redirect_uri is Sensitive
============================

If attackers control redirect_uri:

Authorization codes may be stolen.

============================
OAuth Vulnerabilities
============================

OAuth implementations frequently suffer from:

• CSRF
• Open redirects
• Token leakage
• State misuse
• Improper validation

============================
State Parameter
============================

OAuth uses:

state

To prevent:

CSRF attacks.

============================
What is the state Parameter?
============================

A random value generated before authentication.

Returned after login.

============================
Why state is Important
============================

Prevents attackers from forcing users into malicious OAuth flows.

============================
CSRF in OAuth
============================

If state is missing or not validated:

Attackers may perform:

Cross-Site Request Forgery.

============================
Forced OAuth Profile Linking
============================

An attacker authenticates with their own OAuth account.

Then tricks the victim into completing the flow.

Result:

Victim account becomes linked to attacker-controlled OAuth identity.

============================
OAuth Account Takeover
============================

A dangerous consequence of OAuth misconfiguration.

============================
Redirect URI Manipulation
============================

Attackers attempt to change:

redirect_uri

To their own domain.

============================
Why This is Dangerous
============================

Authorization codes get sent to attacker-controlled servers.

============================
Example Redirect URI Attack
============================

Legitimate:

https://app.com/callback

Attacker changes to:

https://evil.com/callback

============================
redirect_uri Validation Failures
============================

Some applications validate poorly.

Examples:

• Prefix matching only
• Substring matching
• Weak wildcard rules

============================
redirect_uri Bypass Techniques
============================

Attackers use:

• Subdomain tricks
• URL encoding
• Path traversal
• Open redirects
• HTML injection

============================
Subdomain Bypass Example
============================

Application allows:

*.example.com

Attacker controls:

evil.example.com

============================
Authorization Code Leakage
============================

Authorization codes may leak through:

• Referer headers
• Browser history
• Logs
• Analytics systems

============================
Referer Header Leakage
============================

If redirected to external pages:

The Referer header may contain authorization codes.

============================
OAuth Implicit Flow
============================

An older OAuth flow.

Now considered:

Deprecated.

============================
Why Implicit Flow is Dangerous
============================

Access tokens are returned directly in:

URL fragments.

============================
Problems with URL Fragment Tokens
============================

Tokens may leak via:

• Browser history
• Logging systems
• Browser extensions
• Referer exposure

============================
PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange)
============================

A modern OAuth security enhancement.

============================
Why PKCE Exists
============================

Protects authorization codes from interception.

Especially important for:

• Mobile apps
• Public clients
• Single-page applications

============================
How PKCE Works
============================

The client generates:

• code_verifier
• code_challenge

The authorization server verifies them during token exchange.

============================
Benefits of PKCE
============================

Even if attackers steal authorization codes:

They cannot exchange them without the verifier.

============================
OAuth Account Linking Risks
============================

Applications often trust:

Email addresses returned by OAuth providers.

============================
Email Verification Problems
============================

If the provider does not verify emails:

Attackers may create accounts using victim emails.

============================
Resulting Risk
============================

Potential:

Account takeover.

============================
OAuth Scope Misconfiguration
============================

Applications sometimes request:

Excessive permissions.

============================
Why Excessive Scopes are Dangerous
============================

Compromised tokens gain:

More privileges.

============================
Token Expiry
============================

OAuth tokens should expire quickly.

============================
Why Token Expiry Matters
============================

Limits attacker access if tokens leak.

============================
Refresh Tokens
============================

Used to obtain new access tokens.

Without forcing re-login.

============================
Refresh Token Risks
============================

Refresh tokens are highly sensitive.

If stolen:

Attackers may maintain long-term access.

============================
OAuth Security Checklist
============================

============================
1. Validate state Parameter
============================

Prevent CSRF.

============================
2. Strict redirect_uri Validation
============================

Allow exact matches only.

============================
3. Use PKCE
============================

Especially for public clients.

============================
4. Limit Scopes
============================

Use least privilege.

============================
5. Short Token Expiry
============================

Reduce token abuse.

============================
6. Verify Email Addresses
============================

Prevent account takeover.

============================
7. Protect Refresh Tokens
============================

Store securely.

============================
OAuth Testing Methodology
============================

Security testers commonly check:

• Missing state parameter
• redirect_uri bypasses
• Token leakage
• Scope escalation
• Open redirects
• PKCE enforcement

============================
PortSwigger OAuth Labs
============================

The labs teach:

• OAuth CSRF
• Implicit flow attacks
• redirect_uri manipulation
• Account takeover

============================
OAuth in Modern Applications
============================

OAuth is heavily used in:

• Web applications
• APIs
• Mobile apps
• Cloud platforms
• Single sign-on systems

============================
Why OAuth Security Matters
============================

OAuth vulnerabilities often result in:

• Full account takeover
• Authentication bypass
• Sensitive data exposure

Because OAuth integrates deeply into authentication systems, vulnerabilities may impact entire user accounts.

============================
Importance in Cybersecurity
============================

Understanding OAuth security helps professionals:

• Secure authentication systems
• Protect access tokens
• Prevent account takeover
• Test modern SSO implementations

OAuth security knowledge is highly valuable in:

• Web Application Security
• API Security
• Cloud Security
• Identity and Access Management (IAM)
• Bug Bounty Hunting

Since modern applications increasingly rely on third-party authentication providers, OAuth security remains one of the most important topics in modern cybersecurity.
keyPoints: OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework widely used for authentication
Authorization Code Flow is the most secure OAuth flow
OAuth uses access tokens instead of passwords
The state parameter prevents CSRF attacks
redirect_uri validation failures may leak authorization codes
OAuth account linking vulnerabilities may lead to account takeover
Implicit Flow is deprecated due to token exposure risks
PKCE protects authorization codes from interception
Scopes should follow least privilege principles
Refresh tokens require strong protection
OAuth vulnerabilities commonly affect modern web and API applications
OAuth security testing is critical in modern cybersecurity

content.theory:
Session management is one of the most important areas in web application security.

Even if authentication is implemented securely, weak session management can still allow attackers to hijack user accounts.

Modern web applications rely heavily on sessions to:

• Maintain user login state
• Track authenticated users
• Store temporary user information
• Manage access control

Because HTTP is stateless, applications use sessions to remember users between requests.

Session management vulnerabilities remain one of the most exploited vulnerability categories in real-world attacks.

Advanced session attacks go beyond simple cookie theft and include:

• Session fixation
• Session puzzling
• Logout bypasses
• Concurrent session abuse
• Cross-subdomain attacks
• Session timeout failures

Understanding advanced session management is critical for modern web security testing.

============================
What is a Session?
============================

A session is:

A server-side mechanism used to maintain state between HTTP requests.

============================
Why Sessions Exist
============================

HTTP is:

Stateless.

This means each request is independent.

Without sessions:

The server would forget who the user is after every request.

============================
How Sessions Work
============================

Typical session flow:

1. User logs in
2. Server creates session
3. Server generates session ID
4. Session ID sent to browser
5. Browser sends session ID with future requests

============================
What is a Session Token?
============================

A session token uniquely identifies a user's session.

Usually stored inside:

Cookies.

============================
Example Session Cookie
============================

PHPSESSID=abc123xyz

============================
Why Session Tokens are Sensitive
============================

Anyone possessing a valid session token may impersonate the user.

============================
Session Hijacking
============================

Occurs when attackers steal valid session tokens.

============================
Common Session Theft Methods
============================

• XSS
• Network sniffing
• Malware
• Browser compromise
• Session fixation

============================
Secure Session Token Requirements
============================

Session tokens must be:

• Random
• Unpredictable
• Unique
• High entropy

============================
What is Entropy?
============================

Entropy measures:

Randomness.

============================
Why Entropy Matters
============================

Weak session IDs may be:

Guessed or brute-forced.

============================
Recommended Entropy
============================

Modern session tokens should contain:

128 bits or more of entropy.

============================
Poor Session Token Example
============================

sessionid=admin123

Predictable and insecure.

============================
Good Session Token Example
============================

Long random cryptographic value.

============================
Sensitive Data in Tokens
============================

Session tokens should NEVER contain:

• Usernames
• Emails
• Roles
• PII

============================
Why PII in Tokens is Dangerous
============================

Attackers may:

• Enumerate users
• Predict tokens
• Extract sensitive information

============================
Pre-Authentication vs Post-Authentication Sessions
============================

Applications often issue sessions before login.

After authentication:

The session ID should change.

============================
Why Session Regeneration Matters
============================

Prevents:

Session fixation attacks.

============================
What is Session Fixation?
============================

Session fixation occurs when:

Attackers force victims to use attacker-controlled session IDs.

============================
Session Fixation Workflow
============================

1. Attacker obtains valid session ID
2. Attacker tricks victim into using it
3. Victim logs in
4. Session becomes authenticated
5. Attacker reuses same session

============================
Why Session Fixation Works
============================

The application fails to:

Regenerate session IDs after login.

============================
Session Fixation Delivery Methods
============================

Attackers may inject session IDs through:

• URLs
• Cookies
• Hidden fields
• HTTP headers

============================
Fixing Session Fixation
============================

Always regenerate session IDs:

After authentication.

============================
Session Puzzling
============================

A more advanced session vulnerability.

============================
What is Session Puzzling?
============================

Occurs when applications reuse the same session variables across multiple workflows.

============================
Why Session Puzzling is Dangerous
============================

Attackers manipulate session state confusion.

Leading to:

• Authentication bypass
• Workflow bypass
• Privilege escalation

============================
Example of Session Puzzling
============================

Application uses:

session['verified']

For:

• Password reset
• MFA verification
• Admin approval

An attacker may abuse one workflow to bypass another.

============================
Concurrent Sessions
============================

Many applications allow:

Multiple active sessions simultaneously.

============================
Why Concurrent Sessions Matter
============================

If credentials are stolen:

Attackers may remain logged in even after password changes.

============================
Password Change Testing
============================

A critical session security test:

After password change:

Are old sessions invalidated?

============================
Why This Matters
============================

If old sessions remain active:

Attackers maintain persistent access.

============================
Logout Mechanism Testing
============================

Applications should destroy sessions server-side during logout.

============================
Common Logout Mistake
============================

Some applications only:

Delete the browser cookie.

But leave server-side session active.

============================
Logout Bypass Attack
============================

1. Save session token
2. Log out
3. Reinsert old token
4. Test if access still works

============================
If Access Still Works
============================

Logout mechanism is broken.

============================
Session Timeout
============================

Sessions should expire automatically.

============================
Why Session Timeout Matters
============================

Prevents long-term abuse of stolen sessions.

============================
Idle Timeout
============================

Session expires after:

User inactivity.

============================
Absolute Timeout
============================

Session expires after:

Fixed total duration.

Regardless of activity.

============================
Why Both Timeouts are Important
============================

Idle timeout protects inactive users.

Absolute timeout limits long-lived sessions.

============================
Testing Session Timeout
============================

1. Stay inactive
2. Reuse old token
3. Check if session still valid

============================
Cookie Security
============================

Session cookies should use secure attributes.

============================
HttpOnly Flag
============================

Prevents JavaScript access to cookies.

============================
Why HttpOnly Matters
============================

Mitigates:

Session theft via XSS.

============================
Secure Flag
============================

Cookies only sent over:
